## 5.

SUPPLIER SELECTION & QUALIFICATION

### 5.1 Supplier Categorization & Tiering

Suppliers are categorized by strategic importance and performance risk to tailor engagement models:

#### **Tier 1 Strategic Suppliers (32 companies)**

**Definition:** Direct suppliers of major subsystems, long-lead items, or sole-source critical components with program-level impact.

**Examples:**
- Propulsion system integrator (engines, transmissions)
- Avionics suite prime
- Landing gear manufacturer
- Rotor system supplier
- Major composite structure fabricators

**Engagement Model:**
- Executive-level relationship owner (CPO or VP level)
- Quarterly business reviews (QBRs) with joint roadmapping
- Long-term agreements (LTAs) with volume commitments
- Joint investment in capacity expansion, tooling, technology
- Co-location of supplier quality engineers (for highest-risk items)
- Early involvement in design (DFM/DFA collaboration)

**Qualification Requirements:**
- AS9100D or equivalent aerospace quality certification
- Financial health assessment (Dun & Bradstreet rating ≥3A2, or equivalent)
- Demonstrated capability on similar programs (reference checks, audits)
- Capacity assessment and scalability plan to 48 units/year
- Cybersecurity assessment (NIST SP 800-171 for CUI-handling suppliers)
- ITAR registration (if handling export-controlled technical data)

---

#### **Tier 2 Preferred Suppliers (95 companies)**

**Definition:** Direct suppliers of production parts, subassemblies, or services; dual-source candidates for strategic items.

**Examples:**
- Machined structural components
- Electrical/electronic subassemblies
- Hydraulic/pneumatic actuators
- Composite detail parts
- Specialty coatings and treatments

**Engagement Model:**
- Commodity Manager relationship owner
- Monthly or bi-monthly performance reviews
- Standard purchase orders or basic ordering agreements (BOAs)
- Supplier development support (process improvement, quality training)
- Competitive bidding with performance-based re-solicitation

**Qualification Requirements:**
- ISO 9001 minimum; AS9100 preferred
- Demonstrated quality system (process FMEAs, SPC, first article inspection capability)
- Financial stability (credit check, payment history review)
- Capacity to meet rate requirements with defined lead times
- Responsiveness and communication (supplier portal participation)

---

#### **Tier 3 Catalog/Commodity Suppliers (180+ companies)**

**Definition:** Suppliers of standard commercial items, fasteners, raw materials, MRO supplies; low technical risk, high volume.

**Examples:**
- Fasteners (AN/MS standard hardware)
- Raw materials (aluminum plate, bar stock, tubing)
- Consumables (adhesives, sealants, cleaning supplies)
- Cutting tools, shop supplies
- Packaging materials

**Engagement Model:**
- Buyer transactional management
- Automated procurement (ERP-driven POs, vendor-managed inventory for high-runners)
- Competitive bidding or catalog pricing with volume discounts
- Minimal oversight beyond receiving inspection

**Qualification Requirements:**
- Business registration and tax compliance
- Product conformity to specifications (material certs, test reports)
- On-time delivery track record
- Responsive customer service

---

### 5.2 Supplier Selection Process

#### **Phase 1: Market Research & Sourcing Strategy (4–6 weeks)**

**Activities:**
1. **Requirement Definition:** Engineering releases technical specifications, drawing package, quality requirements (ICD-level interface specs for Tier 1)
2. **Market Analysis:** Identify potential suppliers through:
   - Industry databases (ThomasNet, Kompass, aerospace supplier registries)
   - Trade shows and conferences
   - Supplier recommendations from other programs
   - OEM-approved vendor lists (for engines, avionics)
3. **Sourcing Strategy Development:** Determine:
   - Single-source, dual-source, or competitive multi-source approach
   - Domestic vs international sourcing (ITAR, supply chain resilience considerations)
   - Should-cost model (bottom-up cost estimate for negotiation baseline)
4.

**Requirement Definition:** Engineering releases technical specifications, drawing package, quality requirements (ICD-level interface specs for Tier 1)
2. **Market Analysis:** Identify potential suppliers through:
   - Industry databases (ThomasNet, Kompass, aerospace supplier registries)
   - Trade shows and conferences
   - Supplier recommendations from other programs
   - OEM-approved vendor lists (for engines, avionics)
3. **Sourcing Strategy Development:** Determine:
   - Single-source, dual-source, or competitive multi-source approach
   - Domestic vs international sourcing (ITAR, supply chain resilience considerations)
   - Should-cost model (bottom-up cost estimate for negotiation baseline)
4. **Preliminary Supplier Screening:** Narrow field to 3–5 qualified candidates based on:
   - Technical capability alignment
   - Capacity and scalability
   - Financial health and business continuity
   - Geographic and supply chain risk

---

#### **Phase 2: Request for Proposal (RFP) & Evaluation (6–10 weeks)**

**RFP Package Contents:**
- Statement of Work (SOW) with technical specifications
- Bill of Materials (BOM) or parts list with quantities (by production phase)
- Quality requirements (AS9100, NADCAP, specific process specs)
- Delivery schedule and rate ramp expectations
- Contractual terms and conditions template
- Data rights and IP requirements
- Export control requirements (ITAR/EAR compliance)
- Supplier questionnaire (financial, capacity, past performance)

**Evaluation Criteria (Tier 1 Major Subcontracts):**

| Criterion | Weight | Scoring Method |
|-----------|--------|----------------|
| **Technical Approach** | 35% | Proposal evaluation vs SOW; design maturity; risk mitigation plan |
| **Past Performance** | 20% | Reference checks; similar program experience; quality metrics |
| **Cost/Price** | 25% | Should-cost comparison; cost realism; price reasonableness; basis of estimate |
| **Capacity & Schedule** | 10% | Facility tours; production plan; supplier schedule risk assessment |
| **Management & Organization** | 5% | Program management approach; key personnel qualifications; subcontractor management |
| **Financial Health** | 5% | D&B rating; audited financials review; going-concern assessment |

**Evaluation Process:**
1. **Technical Evaluation:** Cross-functional team (Engineering, Quality, Manufacturing, SC) scores proposals
2. **Site Visits:** Top 2–3 candidates; assess facilities, interview personnel, review processes
3. **Cost Analysis:** Finance and Procurement validate cost proposals, conduct cost/price analysis
4. **Best Value Determination:** Integrated assessment; trade-offs between cost and non-cost factors
5. **Competitive Range:** Discussions with top candidates; request Best and Final Offers (BAFOs)
6. **Source Selection:** Document recommendation; executive approval per authority matrix

**Timeline:** 
- RFP release to proposal receipt: 4–6 weeks
- Evaluation and site visits: 3–4 weeks
- Negotiation and award: 1–2 weeks
- **Total: 8–12 weeks** (longer for complex Tier 1 items)

---

#### **Phase 3: Negotiation & Contract Award (2–4 weeks)**

**Negotiation Objectives:**
- **Price:** Target cost within should-cost ±10%; learning curve commitments for multi-year
- **Terms:** Payment terms (net 30 standard; progress payments for long-lead items); warranty (12–24 months standard)
- **Delivery:** Lead time commitments; schedule flexibility; expedite fees; late delivery penalties
- **Quality:** First article inspection (FAI) requirements; process qualification; defect rate targets; corrective action response time
- **IP & Data Rights:** Technical data package ownership; as-built data delivery; design change rights
- **Risk Allocation:** Liability caps; force majeure; material price escalation clauses

**Contract Award:**
- Contracts Manager executes based on negotiated terms
- Kickoff meeting scheduled within 2 weeks (for Tier 1 suppliers)
- Program Management Office (PMO) notified; IMS updated with supplier delivery dates

---

### 5.3 New Supplier Qualification & Onboarding

#### **Qualification Activities (Pre-Production)**

**Technical Qualification:**
1. **First Article Inspection (FAI):** Supplier manufactures and inspects first unit per AS9102 requirements; Eurus Quality Engineer witnesses or reviews
2. **Process Validation:** Critical processes (e.g., heat treat, welding, composite cure) demonstrated and documented (process FMEAs, control plans)
3.

**First Article Inspection (FAI):** Supplier manufactures and inspects first unit per AS9102 requirements; Eurus Quality Engineer witnesses or reviews
2. **Process Validation:** Critical processes (e.g., heat treat, welding, composite cure) demonstrated and documented (process FMEAs, control plans)
3. **Capability Studies:** Statistical process control (SPC) data for critical dimensions; Cpk ≥1.33 target
4. **Tooling Approval:** Production tooling first-article; dimensional validation; tool maintenance plan

**Quality System Assessment:**
1. **Supplier Audit:** On-site audit by Eurus Supplier Quality Engineer; AS9100/ISO 9001 conformance; special process certifications (NADCAP for heat treat, welding, NDT)
2. **Drawing & Specification Review:** Engineering confirms supplier understanding; dimensional tolerances; material certifications; marking/identification
3. **Inspection & Test Plan Approval:** Supplier submits inspection plan; critical characteristics identified; hold points for Eurus witness/approval

**Supply Chain Onboarding:**
1. **Supplier Portal Access:** Training on Eurus supplier portal (order acknowledgment, delivery scheduling, quality notifications)
2. **ERP Integration:** EDI setup for automated PO transmission, ASN (advance ship notice), invoicing (for high-volume suppliers)
3. **Packaging & Shipping Requirements:** Training on Eurus logistics standards; labeling; material handling; transportation modes
4. **Export Compliance:** ITAR/EAR training (if applicable); Technology Control Plan (TCP) acknowledgment; secure data handling

**Timeline for Qualification:**
- **Tier 1 Major Suppliers:** 6–9 months (design collaboration, FAI, audits, process qual)
- **Tier 2 Suppliers:** 3–6 months (FAI, audit, tooling approval)
- **Tier 3 Commodity:** 4–8 weeks (sample approval, basic quality check)

---

### 5.4 Supplier Performance Management (Ongoing)

**Performance Scorecard (Tier 1 & 2 Suppliers):**

| Metric | Weight | Measurement | Target (Green) | Caution (Yellow) | Action Req'd (Red) |
|--------|--------|-------------|----------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | 30% | % of line items delivered on/before committed date | ≥98% | 95–97.9% | <95% |
| **Quality (DPMO)** | 30% | Defects per million opportunities | <300 | 300–1000 | >1000 |
| **Cost Performance** | 15% | Actual cost vs target cost; learning curve achievement | Within ±5% | ±5–10% | >±10% |
| **Responsiveness** | 15% | Avg time to respond to inquiries; issue resolution time | <24 hrs / <7 days | 24–48 hrs / 7–14 days | >48 hrs / >14 days |
| **Continuous Improvement** | 10% | # of cost/quality improvement initiatives; savings delivered | ≥2/year, $50K+ | 1/year | None |

**Review Cadence:**
- **Tier 1:** Monthly scorecard review; quarterly face-to-face QBRs
- **Tier 2:** Quarterly scorecard review; annual business review
- **Tier 3:** Annual review (or event-driven for issues)

**Corrective Action Process:**
- **Yellow Status:** Supplier notified; improvement plan requested within 2 weeks; Commodity Manager monitors
- **Red Status:** Corrective Action Request (CAR) issued; root cause analysis (8D or equivalent) required within 1 week; executive escalation; potential for supplier disqualification if unresolved

### 6.1 Supplier Segmentation & Engagement Models

Building on the tiering in Section 5.1, we employ differentiated relationship strategies:

#### **Strategic Partners (Top 10 Tier 1 Suppliers)**

- Multi-year, multi-program relationships extending beyond MAAP-1
- Joint business planning (capacity, technology roadmaps, cost reduction)
- Co-investment in capabilities (tooling, automation, workforce development)

- **Annual Supplier Summits:** CEO/executive participation; innovation showcases; long-term strategy alignment

- **Co-Location Programs:** Eurus resident engineers at supplier facilities during critical production phases; supplier liaison at Eurus for integration support

**Examples:**

- Rotor system supplier (blades, hubs, controls)

**Performance Expectations:**
- OTD: ≥99%
- Quality: <100 DPMO
- Cost: Annual cost reduction ≥3%
- Innovation: ≥1 new technology/process improvement per year

---

#### **Preferred Suppliers (Tier 1 & Tier 2, ~85 companies)**

**Relationship Characteristics:**
- Program-specific relationships with potential for expansion
- Collaborative problem-solving and continuous improvement
- Regular communication but less strategic depth than partners
- Competitive pressure maintained through dual-sourcing or re-bid options

**Engagement Activities:**
- **Monthly Operational Reviews:** Delivery status; quality issues; upcoming demand; near-term risks
- **Quarterly Performance Reviews:** Scorecard review; CAPA status; improvement initiatives
- **Supplier Development Support:** Eurus-provided training (Lean, Six Sigma, AS9100 prep); process audits with actionable recommendations
- **Cost Reduction Initiatives:** Annual cost reduction targets (3–5%); value engineering workshops

**Examples:**
- Landing gear suppliers
- Major composite structure fabricators
- Hydraulic/pneumatic systems integrators
- Electrical harness manufacturers

**Performance Expectations:**
- OTD: ≥98%
- Quality: <300 DPMO
- Cost: Annual cost reduction ≥2%
- Responsiveness: Issue resolution within 7 days

---

#### **Transactional Suppliers (Tier 3, 180+ companies)**

**Relationship Characteristics:**
- Arms-length, purchase-order-driven transactions
- Minimal customization; commodity or catalog items
- Price and delivery are primary decision factors
- Easily substitutable; competitive re-sourcing common

**Engagement Activities:**
- **Automated Procurement:** ERP-generated POs; minimal human intervention for repeat orders
- **Annual Bid Events:** Competitive re-bidding for contracts >$100K annually; multi-year agreements with price protection for high-volume items
- **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI):** For fasteners, consumables; supplier maintains min/max stock levels at Eurus facility; consignment or periodic billing

**Examples:**
- Fastener distributors (AN/MS hardware)
- Raw material suppliers (aluminum plate, bar, sheet)
- Consumables (adhesives, sealants, shop supplies)
- MRO and tooling supplies

**Performance Expectations:**
- OTD: ≥95%
- Quality: Conformance to specs; zero non-conformances
- Cost: Competitive pricing; annual cost reviews

### 6.2 Supplier Development Programs

For Preferred and Strategic suppliers, Eurus invests in capability building to improve performance and reduce risk:

#### **Lean Manufacturing Training**

**Objective:** Improve supplier efficiency, reduce lead times, eliminate waste

**Approach:**
- Eurus Lean coaches conduct 2-day workshops at supplier facilities (up to 10 suppliers/year)
- Focus areas: 5S, value stream mapping, kanban pull systems, SMED (quick changeover)
- Follow-up audits at 6 months; savings tracked and shared (50/50 split)

**Target Outcomes:**
- Cycle time reduction: 15–25%
- Inventory reduction: 20–30%
- Quality improvement: 10–15% defect reduction

---

#### **AS9100 Preparation & Certification Support**

**Objective:** Elevate Tier 2 suppliers to AS9100 certification (aerospace quality standard)

**Approach:**
- Gap assessment by Eurus Supplier Quality Engineer
- Recommended corrective actions and timeline
- Mock audits and readiness reviews
- Eurus covers 50% of certification costs (up to $25K per supplier) for strategic items

**Target:** Achieve AS9100 certification for 15 Tier 2 suppliers by end of LRIP-3 (2028)

---

#### **Capacity Expansion Support**

**Objective:** Enable supplier capacity to meet FRP rates (48 units/year)

**Approach:**
- Joint capacity assessments during LRIP-2 (2027)
- Eurus provides:
  - Advanced demand forecasts (rolling 18-month firm, 36-month planning)
  - Long-term purchase commitments (multi-year agreements with minimum buys)
  - In some cases, advance payments or tooling investments (amortized into unit price)
- Supplier provides:
  - Detailed capital investment plan (equipment, facility expansion, workforce)
  - Timeline and milestones
  - Cost impact analysis (NRE recovery, learning curve)

**Investment Scale:**
- **Propulsion Supplier:** $85M expansion (supplier-funded with Eurus volume commitment)
- **Composite Structures (2 suppliers):** $45M total (Eurus advances $15M, recovered over 5 years)
- **Avionics Integration:** $28M automation investment (supplier-funded; Eurus provides technical specifications)

**Total Eurus Investment in Supplier Capacity:** ~$35M (direct funding/advances) + ~$450M in purchase commitments enabling external financing

### 6.3 Supplier Innovation & Technology Insertion

**Objective:** Leverage supplier R&D to improve aircraft performance, reduce cost, enhance sustainability

**Mechanisms:**

#### **Technology Roadmap Collaboration**

- Annual workshops with Strategic Partners to align on 3–5 year technology trends
- Focus areas:
  - **Materials:** Advanced composites (OOA cure, thermoplastic), aluminum-lithium alloys, additive manufacturing for complex parts
  - **Manufacturing Processes:** Automated fiber placement, friction stir welding, robotic assembly
  - **Systems:** More-electric architecture (reduced hydraulics), distributed propulsion concepts, AI-based diagnostics

**Example Initiatives:**
- **Composite Supplier:** Transition from autoclave-cured to out-of-autoclave (OOA) prepreg for secondary structures; reduces energy cost 30%, cycle time 40%
- **Avionics Supplier:** Open-architecture mission computer upgrade path; modular hardware refresh without redesigning airframe interfaces

---

#### **Supplier-Funded R&D Partnerships**

- Eurus issues "Innovation Challenges" with defined performance/cost targets
- Suppliers propose solutions; Eurus provides test articles and data sharing
- Successful innovations incorporated into production; supplier retains IP rights but grants Eurus license; cost savings shared (e.g., 60/40 split favoring Eurus)

**LRIP-3 Innovation Challenge Example:**
- **Challenge:** Reduce rotor blade weight by 8% without compromising fatigue life
- **Winner:** Supplier proposed carbon/Kevlar hybrid layup with optimized ply orientation
- **Result:** 10.2% weight reduction achieved; $285K NRE investment by supplier; $1.2M savings over 100 aircraft; 60/40 split = $720K to Eurus, $480K to supplier

### 6.4 Conflict Resolution & Dispute Management

**Escalation Path:**

**Level 1: Working-Level Resolution (Buyer ↔ Supplier Account Manager)**
- Routine delivery or quality issues
- Target resolution: 5 business days
- Tools: Email, phone, supplier portal issue tracking

**Level 2: Management Review (Commodity Manager ↔ Supplier Operations Manager)**
- Unresolved Level 1 issues, cost disputes, schedule conflicts
- Target resolution: 10 business days
- Tools: Formal meetings (in-person or virtual), corrective action plans

**Level 3: Executive Mediation (SC Director ↔ Supplier VP/GM)**
- Performance failures, contract disputes, financial issues
- Target resolution: 20 business days
- Tools: Executive review meetings, third-party mediation (if needed), contract amendment negotiations

**Level 4: Legal & Contractual (CPO + Legal ↔ Supplier Executive + Legal)**
- Breach of contract, liability claims, termination considerations
- Resolution: Per contract dispute resolution clause (negotiation → mediation → arbitration → litigation as last resort)

**Prevention Strategies:**
- Clear, unambiguous contract terms (reviewed by Legal before award)
- Regular communication (prevent surprises)
- Early identification of at-risk items (monthly risk reviews)
- Collaborative problem-solving culture (focus on program success, not blame)

## 7. PROCUREMENT STRATEGY BY MAJOR SUBSYSTEM

This section details the sourcing approach for each major subsystem defined in the Program Requirements Baseline and BOM, linking to IMS procurement schedules and risk mitigation strategies.

### 7.1 Propulsion System (BOM Group 2 & 13)

**Scope:**
- Main engines (2× turboshaft, 2,200 shp each)
- Main transmission (combining gearbox)
- Tail rotor gearbox and driveshaft
- Auxiliary Power Unit (APU)
- Engine accessories (fuel control units, oil systems, fire suppression)

**Total Estimated Value:** ~$2,450M over program (42% of total procurement)

---

#### **7.1.1 Main Engines (AF-MAAP1-PS-1001-00)**

**Current Baseline Assumption:** GE T700-GE-701D or equivalent

**Sourcing Strategy:** **Single-source strategic partnership with qualification of alternate engine**

**Rationale:**
- Engine is the longest-lead item (52+ weeks) and program critical path through LRIP-3
- GE T700 family has proven reliability on military rotorcraft (Apache, Black Hawk); de-risks certification
- Single-source simplifies initial integration, spares logistics, maintenance training
- However, sole-source dependency unacceptable for 350+ unit production run; second engine qualification planned by FRP-1

**Procurement Approach:**

**Phase 1 (LRIP-1 to LRIP-3, 2025–2028):**
- **Supplier:** GE Aviation (or selected OEM post-down-select if competitive source selection conducted)
- **Contract Type:** Fixed-price with economic price adjustment (EPA) for material/labor escalation; multi-year commitment (4 years)
- **Quantities:** 
  - LRIP-1: 4 engines (2 aircraft + 1 test + 1 spare)
  - LRIP-2: 12 engines (6 aircraft)
  - LRIP-3: 24 engines (12 aircraft)
  - **Total: 40 engines** in Phase 1 contract
- **Delivery Schedule:** 
  - First engine delivery: Q3 2025 (for ground test and first flight Q4 2025)
  - Production deliveries: Quarterly batches aligned to aircraft build schedule (IMS WBS 3.2.

2025–2028):**
- **Supplier:** GE Aviation (or selected OEM post-down-select if competitive source selection conducted)
- **Contract Type:** Fixed-price with economic price adjustment (EPA) for material/labor escalation; multi-year commitment (4 years)
- **Quantities:** 
  - LRIP-1: 4 engines (2 aircraft + 1 test + 1 spare)
  - LRIP-2: 12 engines (6 aircraft)
  - LRIP-3: 24 engines (12 aircraft)
  - **Total: 40 engines** in Phase 1 contract
- **Delivery Schedule:** 
  - First engine delivery: Q3 2025 (for ground test and first flight Q4 2025)
  - Production deliveries: Quarterly batches aligned to aircraft build schedule (IMS WBS 3.2.1)
- **Price Commitment:** Learning curve: 90% cumulative average (engines have shallow learning due to high automation)
- **Key Terms:**
  - Warranty: 3,000 hours or 3 years, whichever first
  - Spares support: OEM commits to 2-week AOG (aircraft on ground) delivery for critical parts
  - Data rights: Government Purpose Rights (GPR) for maintenance manuals; OEM retains design IP
  - Foreign Military Sales (FMS): Pre-negotiated terms for allied nation export variants (pricing, delivery)

**Phase 2 (FRP-1 to FRP-2+, 2029–2032+):**
- **Second-Source Qualification Initiated (2027):** Competitive solicitation for alternate engine (e.g., Pratt & Whitney PT6T-9 derivative, Safran Arriel 2, or new design)
- **Qualification Timeline:** 24 months (2027–2029)
  - Requirements definition and RFP: Q1-Q2 2027
  - Proposals and down-select: Q3 2027
  - Integration design (nacelle, fuel system, controls): Q4 2027–Q2 2028
  - Ground test campaign: Q3 2028–Q1 2029
  - Flight test and certification: Q2–Q4 2029
  - Production readiness: Q1 2030
- **Dual-Source Strategy (2030+):**
  - 60/40 split between primary and alternate engine (first aircraft with alternate engine at FRP-2 rate)
  - Competitive bidding for annual lots; price/performance drives allocation
  - Commonality: Engine mounts, fuel system, FADEC interface designed for interchangeability; no airframe changes required
- **Investment:** $45M NRE for second-source integration and qualification (funded by Eurus; amortized into FRP pricing)

**Risk Mitigation:**
- **Supplier Capacity Risk (High Impact):** 
  - GE currently produces ~500 T700 engines/year across all programs; MAAP-1 represents ~10% at FRP rates
  - Mitigation: Early capacity commitment (2025); GE expanding supplier base for critical modules (compressor, turbine); quarterly capacity reviews
- **Technology Obsolescence (Medium Impact):**
  - T700 design is mature (1970s lineage); digital engine controls (FADEC) are current but may see component obsolescence
  - Mitigation: OEM obsolescence management program; Eurus participates in engine user group; upgrade path defined in LTA
- **Export Control (Medium Impact):**
  - Engines subject to ITAR (USML Category VIII(h)); complicates FMS sales
  - Mitigation: Export variant uses FAA-certified commercial derivative (GE CT7) with reduced performance but exportable; pre-approved by State Department

**IMS Linkage:**
- **Critical Path:** Engine delivery is critical path for first 22 aircraft (LRIP-1 through LRIP-3)
- **Float:** Zero float in LRIP-1; 4-week float by LRIP-3 (as manufacturing becomes critical path)
- **Milestones:**
  - Engine contract award: Q2 2025 (IMS MS-1.3)
  - First engine delivery: Q3 2025 (IMS MS-2.1)
  - Second-source qual complete: Q4 2029 (IMS MS-5.2)

#### **7.1.2 Main Transmission & Tail Rotor Gearbox**

**Scope:**
- Main combining gearbox (AF-MAAP1-PS-2001-00): $1,250K unit cost
- Tail rotor gearbox (AF-MAAP1-RS-2003-00): $245K unit cost

**Sourcing Strategy:** **Sole-source with OEM integration partnership**

**Rationale:**
- Transmission is bespoke design specific to MAAP-1 tandem-rotor configuration; no COTS equivalent
- High NRE investment ($35M for design, tooling, qualification); only economically viable with single supplier amortizing across full production run
- GE, Safran, ZF Luftfahrttechnik, or similar have core competency; limited supplier base

**Procurement Approach:**

**Supplier Selection (2024–2025):**
- Competitive RFP issued Q1 2024 to 4 qualified bidders
- Evaluation criteria: 40% technical (design maturity, weight, efficiency), 30% cost (NRE + recurring), 20% schedule, 10% past performance
- Down-select and contract award: Q3 2024 (completed; assumed Supplier X for this plan)

**Contract Structure:**
- **Type:** Cost-Plus-Fixed-Fee (CPFF) for NRE phase (design, tooling, first article); transitions to Fixed-Price-Incentive-Fee (FPIF) for production
- **NRE Phase (2024–2026):** $35M budget
  - Design and analysis: $12M
  - Tooling and test rigs: $18M
  - Qualification testing (durability, performance): $5M
- **Production Phase (2026–2032):**
  - LRIP-1 unit cost: $1,450K (main transmission); 88% learning curve to FRP-2 target of $925K
  - Payment terms: 30% advance upon PO (to fund long-lead castings/forgings), 60% upon delivery, 10% retention released after 500-hour endurance test on first aircraft

**Risk Mitigation:**
- **Design Maturity Risk (High Impact):** Transmission is clean-sheet design; risk of performance shortfalls or weight growth
  - Mitigation: Phased design reviews (PDR, CDR) with independent expert review; test rig validation before first flight; weight contingency 8% at CDR
- **Single-Source Dependency (Medium Impact):** No backup supplier if Supplier X fails
  - Mitigation: Escrow tooling and design data; contractual clause allowing Eurus to transfer production if Supplier X defaults; financial health monitoring quarterly
- **Long Lead Time (High Impact):** 40-week lead time for housings (magnesium castings); critical path item
  - Mitigation: 6-month buffer inventory for castings by LRIP-3; dual-source casting suppliers (domestic + international)

### 7.2 Rotor Systems (BOM Group 2 - Rotor Assemblies)

#### **7.2.1 Main Rotor Blades & Hub (AF-MAAP1-RS-1001/1002)**

**Current Baseline:** 5-blade rotor; carbon fiber/titanium hybrid spar construction

**Sourcing Strategy:** **Single-source with second-source development plan**

**Rationale:**
- Rotor blades are safety-critical, flight-performance-defining components; require extensive certification testing
- High NRE investment ($28M for blade design, tooling, whirl tower testing, flight test)
- Supplier base limited to specialist rotorcraft manufacturers (UTC/Sikorsky, Bell, Leonardo, Airbus Helicopters)

**Procurement Approach:**

**Phase 1: Single-Source Award (2024–2029)**
- **Supplier:** Bell Helicopter (assumed; or competitive down-select)
- **Scope:** Main rotor hub ($485K/unit) + 5-blade set ($750K/unit) = $1.235M per aircraft
- **Contract Type:** CPFF for NRE (2024–2025), FPIF for LRIP (2026–2028), FFP for FRP (2029+)
- **NRE Investment:** $28M (Eurus-funded)
  - Blade design & analysis: $8M
  - Tooling (composite layup mandrels, cure fixtures): $12M
  - Testing (static, fatigue, whirl tower, flight test blades): $8M
- **Production Learning Curve:** 87% (composite-intensive processes have good learning)
- **Delivery Schedule:**
  - First article blades: Q2 2025 (for ground test rig)
  - Flight-worthy blades: Q4 2025 (for first flight)
  - Production deliveries: 12-week lead time; batches of 10 blade sets

**Phase 2: Second-Source Qualification (2028–2030)**
- **Trigger:** FRP gate (2029); hedge against single-source risk and enable price competition
- **Approach:** 
  - License Bell design to second supplier (e.g., Spirit AeroSystems, Premium Aerotec) OR competitive solicitation for alternate blade design meeting same performance spec
  - Eurus funds qualification testing for second source: $8M
  - Second source achieves production readiness by Q4 2030 (FRP-2)
- **Split Strategy (2031+):** 70/30 allocation (primary/alternate); annual re-compete for price/performance

**Risk Mitigation:**
- **Certification Risk (High Impact):** Rotor blades must pass FAA Part 29 airworthiness, including bird strike, fatigue (20,000-hour life), lightning strike
  - Mitigation: Early engagement with FAA; experienced supplier with certification history; conservative design margins; extensive testing program
- **Supplier Capacity (Medium Impact):** Bell currently produces ~600 blade sets/year across programs; MAAP-1 adds ~50/year at FRP
  - Mitigation: Capacity assessment complete (Q1 2025); Bell commits to dedicated production line by 2028; quarterly capacity reviews
- **Damage/FOD Risk (Medium Impact):** Blades susceptible to foreign object damage (FOD), erosion; high repair/replacement cost
  - Mitigation: Leading-edge erosion protection (titanium or nickel abrasion strips); field repair kits; spares pool (15% of fleet)

#### **7.2.2 Rotor Control Actuators (Servo Actuators)**

**Scope:** 
- Cyclic actuators (3× per aircraft): AF-MAAP1-RS-3010-00 ($95K each)
- Collective actuator (1× per aircraft): AF-MAAP1-RS-3011-00 ($95K)
- Tail rotor actuator (1× per aircraft): AF-MAAP1-RS-3012-00 ($95K)
- **Total: 5 actuators per aircraft; ~$475K per aircraft**

**Sourcing Strategy:** **Dual-source from onset**

**Rationale:**
- Servo actuators are mature, safety-critical components with established suppliers (Moog, Parker Hannifin, Safran, UTC Aerospace)
- Dual-sourcing reduces supply risk and enables price competition
- Design specifications are well-defined (force, stroke, frequency response, redundancy); low customization

**Procurement Approach:**

**Supplier Selection (Completed Q2 2024):**
- Competitive RFP to 5 qualified suppliers
- Award to **Primary Supplier: Moog Inc.** (60% allocation) and **Alternate Supplier: Parker Hannifin** (40% allocation)
- Contract Type: FFP with multi-year pricing; annual re-allocation based on performance scorecard

**Qualification:**
- Both suppliers provide first articles (Q3 2024)
- Qualification testing (environmental, fatigue, failure modes): Q4 2024–Q1 2025
- Production release: Q2 2025

**Delivery & Pricing:**
- Lead time: 20 weeks
- LRIP-1 unit cost: $105K (Moog), $108K (Parker); learning to $82K (Moog), $85K (Parker) by FRP-2
- Volume commitment: Forecast provided 12 months rolling; firm orders 6 months out

**Risk Mitigation:**
- **Obsolescence (Medium Impact):** Electronics components (motor drivers, position sensors) subject to obsolescence
  - Mitigation: Suppliers commit to 10-year production support; design refresh option at FRP-2 if components become unavailable; Eurus funds $2M lifetime buy of critical ICs if needed
- **Quality Risk (Medium Impact):** Actuator failures in flight are catastrophic
  - Mitigation: 100% acceptance testing (functional, environmental screening); burn-in period (48 hours); redundant actuator architecture per FAA Part 29

#### **7.3.1 Integrated Modular Avionics (IMA) Core**

**Scope:** AF-MAAP1-AV-1001-00 (dual-redundant IMA; $825K per aircraft for 2 units)

**Sourcing Strategy:** **Open-architecture MOSA-compliant; multi-vendor ecosystem**

**Rationale:**
- Avoid vendor lock-in for mission-critical avionics
- Enable technology refresh without aircraft redesign (per PRB MOSA-1 through MOSA-5 requirements)
- Leverage commercial/military COTS advancements (Moore's Law benefit)

**Procurement Approach:**

**Architecture Standard:** FACE™ (Future Airborne Capability Environment) Technical Standard, Edition 3.1
- Hardware: VITA 65 (OpenVPX) modular slots
- Software: POSIX-compliant operating system; portable application components
- Interfaces: ARINC 653 partitioned OS; MIL-STD-1553B and ARINC 664 (AFDX) data buses

**Supplier Ecosystem:**
- **Primary Integrator:** Collins Aerospace (or equivalent; competitive selection Q1 2025)
  - Provides: IMA chassis, power supply, backplane, integration services
  - Contract: FFP for hardware; T&M for integration support (capped at $5M/year)
- **Module Suppliers (Competitive):** Processing cards, I/O cards, graphics processors procured separately; plug into IMA chassis
  - Examples: Curtiss-Wright, Mercury Systems, Abaco Systems
  - Strategy: Best-of-breed selection; modules re-competed every 3 years to leverage technology advances

**Technology Refresh Strategy:**
- **LRIP-1 to LRIP-3 (2025–2028):** Baseline IMA with Gen-1 processor modules (quad-core PowerPC or equivalent)
- **FRP-1 (2029):** Processor upgrade to Gen-2 (octa-core, 2× performance); drop-in replacement; no software recompilation required (FACE compliance)
- **FRP-2+ (2030+):** Continuous module upgrades as technology advances; target 3-year refresh cycle

**Risk Mitigation:**
- **Obsolescence (High Impact):** Electronics components have 5–7 year production lifecycles; shorter than 20+ year aircraft service life
  - Mitigation: MOSA architecture designed for modularity; lifetime buy of critical components if needed; emulation/FPGA-based replacements for obsolete chips
- **Integration Risk (Medium Impact):** Multi-vendor ecosystem increases integration complexity
  - Mitigation: Detailed Interface Control Documents (ICDs); integration lab for pre-flight testing; primary integrator responsible for system-level validation
- **Export Control (High Impact):** IMA processes classified algorithms (e.g., GPS anti-jam, secure comms); ITAR Category XI(a)
  - Mitigation: Partitioned architecture; export variants use commercial GPS and non-classified algorithms; software build variants (domestic vs export)

## 7. PROCUREMENT STRATEGY BY MAJOR SUBSYSTEM *(continued)*

### 7.3 Avionics & Mission Systems (BOM Group 6, 11, 12) *(continued)*

#### **7.3.2 Mission Systems (ISR Variant - MAAP-1I)** *(continued)*

**Sourcing Strategy:** **Prime contractor integrator with subcontractor specialists**

**Rationale:**
- ISR mission systems are highly specialized; limited suppliers with appropriate security clearances and technical expertise
- System-of-systems integration complexity requires a prime integrator responsible for total package performance
- Sensor technologies (EO/IR, SAR, SIGINT) are rapidly evolving; flexibility for technology insertion essential
- Export control complexity (ITAR Category XI) requires careful supplier vetting and technology transfer controls

**Procurement Approach:**

**Prime Integrator Selection (Q2 2025):**
- Competitive RFP to qualified ISR system integrators (L3Harris, Northrop Grumman, Collins Aerospace, Raytheon, or equivalent)
- Evaluation Criteria:
  - 35% Technical (sensor performance, integration approach, growth capacity)
  - 25% Cost (NRE + recurring, lifecycle support costs)
  - 20% Past Performance (similar ISR integration programs)
  - 10% Schedule (delivery alignment with MAAP-1I variant IOC: Q4 2027)
  - 10% Management (teaming, subcontractor management, risk mitigation)

**Contract Structure:**
- **Prime Contract Type:** Cost-Plus-Award-Fee (CPAF) for NRE and LRIP; transitions to FPIF for FRP
- **NRE Phase (2025–2027):** $78M budget
  - System architecture and integration design: $18M
  - Software development (mission management, sensor fusion, data exploitation): $32M
  - Integration lab and ground testing: $15M
  - Flight test integration and demonstration: $13M
- **Production Phase (2027–2032):**
  - LRIP unit cost: $18.9M per ISR mission package (sensors + processing + operator stations)
  - Learning curve: 91% (software-intensive systems have flatter learning)
  - FRP-2 target cost: $14.2M per package (25% reduction through software reuse, supplier learning, competitive sensor re-procurement)

**Subcontractor Management:**
- Prime integrator responsible for sensor subcontractor selection, with Eurus approval rights for strategic sensors (EO/IR turret, SAR)
- Preferred subcontractors (based on market assessment):
  - **EO/IR Turret:** FLIR Systems/Teledyne, L3Harris WESCAM
  - **SAR:** Northrop Grumman (Lynx family), Leonardo (PicoSAR), Telephonics
  - **SIGINT:** Exelis/L3Harris, BAE Systems
- All sensor ICDs reviewed and approved by Eurus Avionics IPT to ensure MOSA compliance (ICD-003)

**Technology Refresh Strategy:**
- **Sensor Refresh Cycle:** 5 years (aligned with DoD sensor technology refresh programs)
- **LRIP (2027–2028):** Gen-1 sensors (current technology baseline)
- **FRP-1 (2029):** Gen-1.5 sensors (running design changes for improved resolution, reduced weight)
- **FRP-2+ (2030+):** Gen-2 sensors (next-generation focal plane arrays, AI-enabled target recognition)
- Modular sensor bay architecture allows sensor upgrades without airframe modifications

**Risk Mitigation:**
- **Integration Risk (High Impact):** Multi-vendor sensor suite with complex data fusion requirements
  - Mitigation: Early integration lab standup (Q3 2025); iterative integration testing; government oversight via Combined Test Team; prime integrator responsible for system-level performance
- **Technology Obsolescence (High Impact):** Electronics obsolescence in 5–7 year timeframe

#### **7.3.3 Navigation & Communication Systems**

**Scope:**
- GPS/INS (AF-MAAP1-AV-2001-00): $185K per aircraft
- VHF/UHF radios (2 units): $95K total

- **Total navigation/comms: ~$850K per aircraft**

**Sourcing Strategy:** **COTS/MOTS procurement with competitive selection**

**Rationale:**
- Navigation and communication systems are mature technologies with established military/commercial suppliers
- Modular architecture allows best-of-breed selection and competitive refresh
- Open standards (ARINC, MIL-STD-1553B, STANAG) enable multi-vendor interoperability

**Procurement Approach:**

**GPS/INS:**
- **Strategy:** Competitive selection from qualified vendors (Honeywell, Northrop Grumman LN-200, Collins, Kearfott)
- **Requirement:** Dual-redundant, embedded GPS/INS (EGI), mil-spec GPS M-code receiver, FAA TSO-C129a certified
- **Contract Type:** FFP multi-year agreement (5 years, LRIP through FRP-2)
- **Supplier:** Down-select Q1 2025; first delivery Q3 2025
- **Obsolescence Plan:** Technology refresh at 7-year intervals; GPS modernization (GPS III compatibility) incorporated by design

**Radios (VHF/UHF):**
- **Strategy:** Dual-source from onset; government-approved suppliers only (NSA Type-1 encryption)
- **Suppliers:** Collins ARC-210, L3Harris AN/ARC-164 (or equivalent)
- **Contract Type:** FFP; annual re-compete for price/delivery performance
- **Requirement:** HAVE QUICK II, SATURN frequency-hopping, AES-256 encryption
- **Lead Time:** 16 weeks; commodity item with established supply base

**SATCOM:**
- **Strategy:** Single-source with technology insertion roadmap
- **Supplier:** Viasat or Inmarsat (SwiftBroadband-Safety certified airborne terminal)
- **Contract Type:** CPFF for airframe integration (antenna, RF design); FFP for recurring hardware
- **Bandwidth:** Minimum 432 kbps downlink, 128 kbps uplink (supports real-time ISR data exfiltration)
- **Export Compliance:** Commercial SATCOM terminal (non-ITAR); encryption handled separately via Type-1 device

**Tactical Datalink (Link 16):**
- **Strategy:** Government-furnished equipment (GFE) or contractor-procured with government approval
- **Suppliers:** L3Harris MIDS-LVT, Viasat Link 16 terminals
- **Contract Type:** FFP if contractor-procured; GFE if government provides from existing inventory
- **Requirement:** MIDS-JTRS or Link 16 compliant; NATO STANAG 5516 for coalition interoperability
- **Security:** Requires COMSEC keying material and Crypto Ignition Keys (CIK) managed separately under EKMS procedures

**Risk Mitigation:**
- **ITAR/Export Risk (Medium Impact):** Tactical datalink and secure radios subject to strict export controls
  - Mitigation: Export variants use commercial radios (no Type-1 encryption);

encryption handled separately via Type-1 device

**Tactical Datalink (Link 16):**

Triumph, GKN Aerospace, Premium Aerotec)
- **Awarded to:** Spirit AeroSystems (assumed for this plan)

**Contract Structure:**
- **NRE Investment:** $42M (Eurus-funded)
  - Composite layup tooling (mandrels, autoclaves, cure fixtures): $28M
  - Process development and first article qualification: $9M
  - Facility modifications (autoclave expansion, cleanroom): $5M
- **Production Contract:**
  - Type: FPIF transitioning to FFP at FRP gate
  - LRIP unit cost (forward fuselage barrel): $285K; FRP target: $175K (39% reduction via learning curve and automation)
  - Learning curve: 85% (labor-intensive composite layup benefits significantly from workforce learning)
  - Delivery lead time: 22 weeks (including cure cycles, NDT, final inspection)

**Manufacturing Process:**
- Hand layup and automated fiber placement (AFP) for large surfaces
- Autoclave cure (8-hour cycle per major assembly)
- Ultrasonic NDT (100% coverage of primary structure)
- Dimensional inspection via laser tracker (±0.015" tolerance on assembly interfaces)

**Tooling Ownership & Risk Sharing:**
- Eurus owns all production tooling (amortized into unit pricing)
- Tooling escrowed at third-party facility; Eurus retains right to transfer production if Spirit defaults
- Spirit responsible for tool maintenance and repair; life-of-program warranty on tooling performance

**Technology Insertion Opportunity (FRP-2+):**
- Transition from autoclave cure to **Out-of-Autoclave (OOA)** prepreg for secondary structures
  - Benefits: 40% energy savings, 35% cycle time reduction, eliminates autoclave capacity constraint
  - Investment: $8M for OOA-compatible tooling and process qualification
  - Timeline: Initiate development Q2 2028; production readiness Q1 2030
  - Risk: OOA composites have shorter certification history; early FAA engagement required

**Risk Mitigation:**
- **Material Obsolescence (Medium Impact):** Prepreg formulations change over time; AS4/8552 is current aerospace standard but may see running changes
  - Mitigation: Multi-year supply agreements with frozen specifications; material qualification testing for any supplier-initiated changes; lifetime buy option if discontinuation threatened
- **Supplier Capacity (High Impact):** Spirit currently operates autoclaves at 75% capacity utilization; MAAP-1 FRP adds 15% load
  - Mitigation: Capacity commitment in contract; Spirit investment in 4th autoclave by Q4 2027 (capital: $12M, Spirit-funded with Eurus volume guarantee)
- **Quality/Defect Risk (High Impact):** Composite defects (porosity, delamination, foreign object debris) can escape visual inspection
  - Mitigation: 100% ultrasonic NDT; process control (SPC) on critical parameters (cure temperature, vacuum pressure); resident Eurus Quality Engineer at Spirit facility during LRIP

**IMS Linkage:**
- Composite structure delivery is on critical path for aircraft #1–#10 (LRIP-1 and early LRIP-2)
- Float increases to 3 weeks by aircraft #22 (LRIP-3) as final assembly becomes dominant constraint
- Milestone: First composite barrel delivery Q4 2025 (IMS MS-2.3)

#### **7.4.2 Aluminum Primary Structure (Machined Components)**

**Scope:**
- Machined bulkheads, frames, fittings (aluminum 7075-T6, 7050-T7451)

**Make-vs-Buy Breakdown:**

**MAKE (In-House Machining: ~35% by part count):**
- Simple brackets, angles, clips (2–4 axis CNC)
- High-volume frames and stringers (family mold approach)
- **Rationale:** High volume justifies internal capacity; short lead times; design flexibility for ECOs
- **Capacity:** 12 CNC mills (5-axis), 8 lathes; capacity for ~30,

frames, fittings (aluminum 7075-T6, 7050-T7451)
- Landing gear attachment fittings (high-strength forgings)
- Engine mount structure
- Total aluminum machined parts: ~3,500 unique part numbers, ~85,000 lb per aircraft

**Sourcing Strategy:** **Hybrid: in-house machining for high-volume, low-complexity; outsource for complex multi-axis and forgings**

**Make-vs-Buy Breakdown:**

**MAKE (In-House Machining: ~35% by part count):**
- Simple brackets, angles, clips (2–4 axis CNC)
- High-volume frames and stringers (family mold approach)
- **Rationale:** High volume justifies internal capacity; short lead times; design flexibility for ECOs
- **Capacity:** 12 CNC mills (5-axis), 8 lathes; capacity for ~30,000 parts/year at FRP rates
- **Lead Time:** 3–6 weeks from raw material receipt to finished part

**BUY (External Machining: ~65% by part count):**
- Complex multi-axis machined components (5+ axis, tight tolerances <0.005")
- Large monolithic forgings (landing gear fittings, transmission mounts)
- **Rationale:** Specialized equipment (large-bed 5-axis mills); lower volume doesn't justify capital investment; supplier expertise
- **Suppliers:** Dual-source strategy
  - **Primary (60% allocation):** Precision Castparts Corp (PCC) or similar tier-1 machining house
  - **Alternate (40% allocation):** Regional aerospace machine shops (3–4 suppliers qualified)

**Procurement Approach (External Machining):**

**Forgings:**
- **Supplier:** Alcoa Forgings, Arconic, or Weber Metals (domestic preference for supply chain resilience)
- **Contract Type:** FFP with multi-year volume commitment (5 years)
- **Lead Time:** 26 weeks for initial forgings (die manufacture, forging, heat treat, rough machining); 16 weeks for repeat orders
- **Critical Path Item:** Landing gear attach forgings are longest-lead structural components
  - Mitigation: Order first 3 years' worth of forgings in single lot (Q2 2025 order); buffer inventory strategy
- **Pricing:** LRIP cost $18K–$45K per forging (depending on size/complexity); minimal learning (forging is capital-intensive, not labor-intensive)

**Machined Components:**
- **Contract Type:** Blanket Purchase Agreement (BPA) with call-off orders per aircraft lot
- **Pricing Strategy:** Should-cost model based on material cost + machine time + overhead multiplier; target 88% learning curve LRIP to FRP
- **Quality Requirements:** AS9100D certified suppliers; First Article Inspection (FAI) per AS9102; in-process inspection at critical features
- **Delivery:** Phased deliveries aligned to assembly schedule; 10–14 week lead time for machined components

**Raw Material Supply (Aluminum Plate/Bar):**
- **Strategy:** Direct procurement by Eurus; issued to machining suppliers (Government-Furnished Material model for external shops)
- **Suppliers:** Alcoa, Constellium, Kaiser Aluminum
- **Rationale:** Volume leverage (procure for both in-house and supplier machining); quality control (traceable material certifications); cost reduction (eliminate supplier markup)
- **Inventory:** Managed via ERP MRP module; 6-week buffer stock for long-lead alloys (7050-T7451)

**Risk Mitigation:**
- **Supplier Capacity Risk (Medium Impact):** Machine shop capacity can be constrained during high-demand periods (commercial aerospace upturn)
  - Mitigation: Long-term agreements with capacity reservations; quarterly capacity reviews; alternate supplier qualified and ready to activate
- **Material Traceability (High Impact — Regulatory):** FAA requires full material traceability for primary structure
  - Mitigation: Material certs (heat lot, chemical composition, mechanical properties) tracked in ERP; QR code on each part linking to digital cert repository
- **Tolerance Stack-Up (Medium Impact):** Tight tolerances required for assembly; cumulative tolerance issues can cause fit problems
  - Mitigation: Digital thread from CAD → CNC → CMM inspection; laser tracker verification at assembly; design tolerances include allocation for process variation (GD&T per ASME Y14.5)

## 8. CRITICAL ITEMS & LONG-LEAD PROCUREMENT

### 8.1 Definition of Critical Items

For the purposes of this procurement plan, **Critical Items** are defined as parts, materials, or systems meeting one or more of the following criteria:

1. **Schedule-Critical:** Item delivery is on the program critical path (zero or negative float in IMS); late delivery directly delays aircraft delivery or program milestones
2. **Single-Source / Sole-Source:** No qualified alternate supplier exists or can be developed within program schedule constraints
3. **Long Lead Time:** Procurement lead time exceeds 26 weeks (6 months), requiring advance commitment before firm production orders
4. **High Technical Risk:** Item involves unproven technology, first-time qualification, or significant certification risk
5. **High Financial Value:** Item represents >5% of aircraft recurring flyaway cost (~$1M+ per aircraft at FRP rates)

### 8.3 Long-Lead Procurement Strategy

**Objective:** Ensure critical items are ordered sufficiently in advance to support program schedule, while minimizing inventory carrying costs and financial risk from early commitment.

**Strategy Components:**

#### **8.3.1 Advance Procurement Planning**

**Timeline:**
- **T-18 months:** Initiate competitive source selection for long-lead items (engines, transmission, landing gear) ✓ *Completed for LRIP-1*
- **T-12 months:** Award contracts; authorize supplier tooling investment and capacity reservation
- **T-6 months:** Firm production orders for items with >24-week lead time
- **T-3 months:** Firm orders for items with 12–24 week lead time
- **Rolling:** Standard procurement for items <12 weeks (MRP-driven)

#### **8.3.2 Buffer Inventory Strategy**

For items where lead time exceeds production cycle time, establish strategic buffer inventory:

| Item Category | Buffer Strategy | Rationale |
|---------------|----------------|-----------|
| **Engines** | 2-unit buffer (1 test, 1 production spare) | Covers supplier delivery variance; enables test program continuity |
| **Transmissions** | 1-unit buffer | Long lead time (40 weeks); critical path mitigation |
| **Landing Gear Forgings** | 3-year forward buy (first order, Q2 2025) | Forging die NRE amortization; locks in supplier capacity |
| **Carbon Fiber Prepreg** | 30-day consignment stock (vendor-managed) | Shelf-life constraint (12 months frozen); JIT delivery risk mitigation |
| **Composite Fuselage Barrels** | Zero buffer (pull system from supplier) | Supplier holds WIP inventory;

3.2 Buffer Inventory Strategy**

For items where lead time exceeds production cycle time, establish strategic buffer inventory:

| Item Category | Buffer Strategy | Rationale |
|---------------|----------------|-----------|
| **Engines** | 2-unit buffer (1 test, 1 production spare) | Covers supplier delivery variance; enables test program continuity |
| **Transmissions** | 1-unit buffer | Long lead time (40 weeks); critical path mitigation |
| **Landing Gear Forgings** | 3-year forward buy (first order, Q2 2025) | Forging die NRE amortization; locks in supplier capacity |
| **Carbon Fiber Prepreg** | 30-day consignment stock (vendor-managed) | Shelf-life constraint (12 months frozen); JIT delivery risk mitigation |
| **Composite Fuselage Barrels** | Zero buffer (pull system from supplier) | Supplier holds WIP inventory; Eurus takes delivery upon acceptance |
| **Avionics LRUs** | 10% buffer pool (shared across aircraft) | Technology refresh flexibility; covers infant mortality failures |

**Total Buffer Inventory Investment:** ~$18M peak (LRIP-3); reduces to ~$8M at FRP (as production flow stabilizes)

#### **8.3.3 Supplier Capacity Reservation**

For critical suppliers (engines, transmission, major composites):
- **Mechanism:** Long-term agreements (LTAs) with minimum-buy commitments and capacity reservation fees
- **Example:** GE engine LTA commits Eurus to minimum 40 engines over 4 years (LRIP phases); GE reserves production line capacity and commits to delivery windows
- **Cost:** Capacity reservation typically 3–5% premium vs spot pricing; justified by schedule assurance

#### **8.3.4 Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Analysis**

For high-volume, long-lead commodity items (fasteners, raw materials), balance order frequency vs carrying cost:

**Example: Aluminum 7075-T6 Plate (annual consumption FRP: 45,000 lb)**

- **Order Setup Cost:** $1,200 per order (procurement admin, incoming inspection, logistics)
- **Carrying Cost:** 18% annual (cost of capital, warehouse, obsolescence risk)
- **Material Cost:** $12/lb
- **EOQ Formula:** √(2 × Annual Demand × Order Cost / Carrying Cost per Unit)
- **Calculated EOQ:** ~8,500 lb per order → ~5 orders per year → ~70-day supply per order

**Procurement Strategy:** Quarterly orders (4× per year) with supplier-managed consignment inventory at Eurus facility; balances carrying cost vs order efficiency

**Risk Color Codes:**
- **Green:** Risk within acceptable tolerance; mitigation plan in place and effective
- **Yellow:** Risk requires active management; mitigation plan in execution; monitor closely
- **Red:** Risk exceeds tolerance; escalation required; may impact program cost/schedule baseline

**Escalation Process:**
- **Yellow Status:** Commodity Manager briefs SC Director weekly; mitigation actions tracked
- **Red Status:** Immediate escalation to Program Manager; CCB notification; may require contract modification, alternate sourcing, or design change

## 9. CONTRACTING STRATEGY & APPROACH

### 9.1 Contract Type Selection Philosophy

Contract type selection balances risk allocation between Eurus Systems and suppliers, considering technical maturity, cost predictability, and program phase:

| Phase | Preferred Contract Type | Rationale |
|-------|------------------------|-----------|
| **NRE / Development** | Cost-Plus-Fixed-Fee (CPFF) or Cost-Plus-Award-Fee (CPAF) | Technical uncertainty; design iteration expected; cost risk appropriately borne by buyer |
| **LRIP (Low-Rate Initial Production)** | Fixed-Price-Incentive-Fee (FPIF) or FFP with EPA (Economic Price Adjustment) | Transitional phase; cost targets firming but learning curve uncertainty remains; share cost risk |
| **FRP (Full-Rate Production)** | Firm-Fixed-Price (FFP) | Mature design; cost predictable; supplier bears cost risk; drives efficiency |
| **Long-Term Services** | Time-and-Materials (T&M) with Not-To-Exceed (NTE) cap | Variable scope; appropriate for on-site support, engineering services |

### 9.2 Contract Structures by Commodity

| Commodity / System | NRE Contract Type | LRIP Contract Type | FRP Contract Type | Rationale for Structure |
|--------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Propulsion (Engines, Transmission)** | CPFF | FPIF → FFP at Lot 3 | FFP with EPA (fuel, material indices) | High NRE; mature technology but custom integration; EPA protects against commodity volatility |
| **Avionics (IMA, Mission Systems)** | CPAF (award fee tied to performance gates) | FPIF | FFP (with tech refresh clauses) | Software-intensive; performance incentives appropriate; obsolescence clauses protect both parties |
| **Structures (Composites, Machining)** | CPFF (tooling), FFP (raw materials) | FPIF (first articles) → FFP | FFP | Tooling risk borne by Eurus (NRE); production costs predictable with learning;

Transmission)** | CPFF | FPIF → FFP at Lot 3 | FFP with EPA (fuel, material indices) | High NRE; mature technology but custom integration; EPA protects against commodity volatility |
| **Avionics (IMA, Mission Systems)** | CPAF (award fee tied to performance gates) | FPIF | FFP (with tech refresh clauses) | Software-intensive; performance incentives appropriate; obsolescence clauses protect both parties |
| **Structures (Composites, Machining)** | CPFF (tooling), FFP (raw materials) | FPIF (first articles) → FFP | FFP | Tooling risk borne by Eurus (NRE); production costs predictable with learning; FFP drives supplier efficiency |
| **Landing Gear** | CPFF | FPIF | FFP | Certification risk in NRE; production costs firm after qualification |
| **COTS/Catalog Items** | N/A | FFP | FFP | Mature commercial items; no NRE; fixed pricing appropriate |
| **On-Site Engineering Support** | T&M with NTE ceiling | T&M with NTE | T&M with NTE or CPFF (if scope defined) | Variable hours; defined labor rates; NTE protects budget |

### 9.3 Standard Contract Terms & Conditions

All Eurus Systems purchase orders and subcontracts incorporate standard Terms & Conditions tailored to aerospace procurement, derived from FAR/DFARS where applicable (for government-funded programs) or commercial best practices:

#### **9.3.1 Core Terms (All Contracts)**

| Term | Standard Provision | Notes |
|------|-------------------|-------|
| **Payment Terms** | Net 30 days from acceptance of conforming goods/services | Discounts for early payment negotiable (e.g., 2% discount for payment within 10 days) |
| **Progress Payments** | Available for contracts >$5M with >12-month performance period; max 80% of costs incurred | Mitigates supplier cash flow burden on long-lead items; requires cost accounting system approval |
| **Title & Risk of Loss** | Title transfers to Eurus upon delivery FOB destination (Eurus facility) unless otherwise specified | Standard for most items; FOB origin for international shipments with Eurus-arranged logistics |
| **Inspection & Acceptance** | Eurus reserves right to inspect at source (supplier facility) and/or destination; acceptance within 10 business days | Reject clauses allow return of non-conforming items at supplier expense |
| **Warranty** | Minimum 12 months from acceptance; 24 months for flight-critical systems; 36 months for engines/transmission | Covers defects in material and workmanship; does not cover normal wear or misuse |
| **Intellectual Property** | Supplier retains IP rights to pre-existing technology; Eurus receives license for program use; data rights per DFARS 252.227-7013 (Technical Data Rights) or equivalent commercial terms | Negotiable for custom-developed items; Eurus seeks Government Purpose Rights (GPR) or Unlimited Rights where feasible |
| **Export Control Compliance** | Supplier certifies compliance with ITAR/EAR; no export/re-export without authorization; ITAR registration if handling USML items | Mandatory for all suppliers handling controlled technical data |
| **Cybersecurity (CUI Handling)** | Suppliers handling Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) must comply with NIST SP 800-171 or equivalent | Assessed via supplier cybersecurity questionnaire and periodic audits |
| **Flow-Down Clauses** | Applicable government contract clauses (e.g., Buy American Act, Equal Opportunity, Counterfeit Parts Prevention) flow down to subcontractors | Ensures compliance throughout supply chain |

#### **9.3.2 Quality & Certification Clauses**

| Clause | Requirement |
|--------|-------------|
| **AS9100 Compliance** | Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers must maintain AS9100D (or current revision) certification; audits permitted by Eurus |
| **First Article Inspection (FAI)** | Required for all production parts prior to lot acceptance; per AS9102 Form 3 |
| **Traceability** | Material certifications (certs of conformance, test reports, heat lot traceability) required for all primary structure materials |
| **Counterfeit Parts Prevention** | Supplier shall source components only from OEMs or authorized distributors; suspect/counterfeit parts reporting mandatory (AS6081, AS5553) |
| **Non-Conformance Reporting** | Supplier shall notify Eurus within 24 hours of discovering non-conforming material; 8D corrective action report required for major defects |
| **Right of Access** | Eurus and customer (if government program) reserve right to access supplier facilities for audits, source inspections, and technical interchange |

| **Termination for Default** | Eurus may terminate for supplier non-performance (quality, delivery); supplier liable for excess re-procurement costs | Standard remedy for breach |

### 9.4 Pricing & Cost Management Mechanisms

#### **9.4.1 Learning Curve Pricing (LRIP → FRP)**

For labor-intensive items (composites, assembly, machining), contracts incorporate agreed-upon learning curves (typically 85–92% cumulative average slope):

**Example: Composite Fuselage Barrel**
- LRIP-1 Unit Cost (Lot 1, Units 1-4): $285K (baseline)
- Assumed Learning: 85% cumulative average
- FRP-2 Unit Cost (Cumulative Unit 100+): $175K (cost reduced by 39% vs LRIP-1)

**Contract Mechanism:**
- Supplier provides detailed Basis of Estimate (BOE) for LRIP-1 pricing, broken down by labor hours, material, overhead
- Learning curve applied to labor and variable overhead elements (not material cost)
- Pricing for each subsequent lot negotiated based on cumulative quantity and learning formula
- Actual costs tracked; if supplier exceeds learning targets, Eurus may renegotiate or compete for subsequent lots

#### **9.4.2 Economic Price Adjustment (EPA) Clauses**

For long-term contracts (3+ years) and commodities subject to market volatility, EPA clauses adjust pricing based on published indices:

**Example: Engine Contract EPA**
- **Base Price:** $1,425K per engine (LRIP-1 baseline)
- **Adjustment Formula:** 
  - 60% of price tied to labor index (Bureau of Labor Statistics, Aerospace Manufacturing)
  - 25% tied to nickel commodity index (for turbine superalloy materials)
  - 15% fixed (profit, tooling amortization)
- **Adjustment Frequency:** Annual, based on prior 12-month average indices
- **Cap:** ±10% maximum adjustment per year to limit volatility

**Rationale:** Protects both parties from unforeseeable economic changes (inflation, commodity spikes); ensures supplier remains economically viable without windfall profits

#### **9.4.3 Cost Reduction Incentives**

**Shared Savings Programs:**
- Supplier-initiated cost reduction ideas (design changes, process improvements, material substitutions) that achieve measurable savings are eligible for shared savings
- **Split:** 50/50 for first-year savings; 60/40 (Eurus/Supplier) for recurring savings
- **Example:** Supplier proposes alternate fastener that reduces installation time by 15% and costs 8% less → Annual savings $450K → Supplier receives $225K first year, $180K/year thereafter
- **Administration:** Documented via Value Engineering Change Proposal (VECP); approved by Engineering and Supply Chain

**Performance-Based Incentives (FPIF Contracts):**
- Target Cost, Target Profit, Ceiling Price established
- If supplier delivers under target cost, shares savings (e.g., 50/50 split)
- If over target cost, shares overrun up to ceiling price;

### 9.5 Subcontract Administration & Oversight

#### **9.5.1 Subcontract Management Roles**

| Role | Responsibility |
|------|----------------|
| **Commodity Manager** | Contract strategy, supplier selection, pricing negotiation, performance management |
| **Buyer** | PO execution, order acknowledgment tracking, expediting, day-to-day supplier communication |
| **Supplier Quality Engineer** | Quality audits, first article inspection, non-conformance resolution, CAPA oversight |
| **Contracts Administrator** | Contract terms review, modifications, legal compliance, dispute resolution |
| **Engineering (IPT) Liaison** | Technical interface, drawing/specification interpretation, engineering change coordination |

#### **9.5.2 Contract Modification Process**

**Triggers for Contract Modification:**
- Quantity changes (>10% from original order)
- Schedule changes (delivery date shifts >2 weeks)
- Engineering changes affecting supplier scope, cost, or delivery
- Price adjustments (EPA clauses, learning curve re-baselines)
- Terms & Conditions changes

**Process:**
1. **Initiation:** Requesting party (Eurus or supplier) submits change request with impact assessment (cost, schedule, technical)
2. **Review:** Commodity Manager reviews; coordinates with Engineering (technical impact), Manufacturing (schedule impact), Finance (budget impact)
3. **Negotiation:** If cost/schedule impact, negotiate with supplier; document agreement
4. **Approval:** Per dollar authority matrix (Section 3.2); modifications >$500K require SC Director approval
5. **Execution:** Contracts Administrator issues formal contract modification; both parties sign
6. **ERP Update:** Buyer updates purchase order in ERP system; IMS updated if schedule impact

**Turnaround Time Target:** ≤10 business days for routine modifications; ≤3 days for expedited (critical path impact)

#### **9.5.3 Supplier Performance Reviews**

**Frequency:**
- **Tier 1 (Strategic):** Monthly operational reviews + Quarterly business reviews (QBRs)
- **Tier 2 (Preferred):** Quarterly reviews
- **Tier 3 (Transactional):** Annual reviews or event-driven (issues only)

**Review Content:**
- Scorecard metrics (OTD, quality, cost, responsiveness) — see Section 5.4
- Open corrective actions and closure status
- Upcoming demand forecast and capacity alignment
- Risk identification (financial health, capacity constraints, quality trends)
- Continuous improvement initiatives and cost reduction pipeline

**Outputs:**
- Scorecard status (Green/Yellow/Red)
- Action items with owners and due dates
- Corrective Action Requests (CARs) for Red status items
- Supplier development plans (if performance gaps identified)

### 9.6 Dispute Resolution & Legal Remedies

**Escalation Hierarchy (per Section 6.4):**
1. Working-level resolution (Buyer ↔ Supplier Account Manager)
2. Management review (Commodity Manager ↔ Supplier Ops Manager)
3. Executive mediation (SC Director ↔ Supplier VP)
4.

Working-level resolution (Buyer ↔ Supplier Account Manager)
2. Management review (Commodity Manager ↔ Supplier Ops Manager)
3. Executive mediation (SC Director ↔ Supplier VP)
4. Legal/Contractual (CPO + Legal ↔ Supplier Executive + Legal)

**Formal Dispute Resolution Mechanisms (Incorporated in Standard T&Cs):**

**Negotiation (First Resort):**
- Good-faith negotiations between authorized representatives
- Documented in writing; 30-day negotiation window

**Mediation (If Negotiation Fails):**
- Neutral third-party mediator (e.g., JAMS, AAA Commercial Mediation)
- Non-binding; costs split 50/50
- 60-day mediation window

**Arbitration (If Mediation Fails):**
- Binding arbitration per American Arbitration Association (AAA) Commercial Arbitration Rules
- Single arbitrator for disputes <$1M; three-arbitrator panel for >$1M
- Venue: [Location specified in contract, typically Eurus headquarters jurisdiction]
- Costs: Each party bears own legal costs; arbitrator fees split unless arbitrator awards otherwise
- Arbitration award is final and binding; enforceable in court

**Litigation (Last Resort):**
- Preserved right to litigate if arbitration clause waived or inapplicable
- Jurisdiction and venue per contract terms (typically Eurus headquarters state/federal court)

**Preference:** Disputes are rare in well-managed supply chains; emphasis on prevention through clear contracts, regular communication, and collaborative problem-solving.

## 10. SUPPLY CHAIN RISK MANAGEMENT

### 10.1 Risk Management Framework

Supply chain risk management is integrated into the overall Program Risk Management Plan (RISK-0001) and follows a structured process:

```
IDENTIFY → ASSESS → MITIGATE → MONITOR → REPORT
```

**Ownership:**
- **Supply Chain Director:** Overall accountability for supply chain risk management
- **Commodity Managers:** Identify and manage risks within their commodity portfolios
- **Program Risk Manager:** Integration of supply chain risks into program-level risk register
- **Risk Management Board:** Forum for cross-functional risk review and mitigation decision-making (monthly meetings)

### 10.2 Risk Categories & Taxonomy

Supply chain risks are categorized using a standard taxonomy for consistent identification and reporting:

| Risk Category | Description | Examples |
|---------------|-------------|----------|
| **Supplier Performance** | Risks related to supplier inability to meet quality, delivery, or cost commitments | Late deliveries, quality defects, cost overruns, supplier financial distress |
| **Technical / Design** | Risks related to technical feasibility, design maturity, or integration challenges | First-time design failures, requirement changes impacting supplier, certification delays |
| **Capacity / Scalability** | Risks related to supplier ability to scale production to meet program ramp | Insufficient production capacity, workforce shortages, capital equipment constraints |
| **Single-Source / Dependency** | Risks related to lack of alternate suppliers or supply chain bottlenecks | Sole-source items, proprietary technology, limited supplier base |
| **Geopolitical / External** | Risks external to supplier control (natural disasters, trade restrictions, pandemics) | Export control changes, tariffs, supply chain disruptions (e.g., port strikes), force majeure events |
| **Technology Obsolescence** | Risks related to component end-of-life or technology becoming unavailable | Electronics obsolescence, supplier discontinuing product lines |
| **Cost / Economic** | Risks related to cost volatility, inflation, or economic downturns affecting suppliers | Commodity price spikes, currency fluctuations (for international suppliers), supplier bankruptcy |
| **Quality / Certification** | Risks related to quality system failures or certification delays | AS9100 decertification, FAA/EASA audit findings, counterfeit parts |
| **Cybersecurity / IP** | Risks related to data breaches, IP theft, or cyber attacks on suppliers | Ransomware attacks disrupting supplier operations, IP theft of proprietary designs |

### 10.3 Risk Assessment Methodology

Each identified risk is assessed using a standard 5×5 Risk Matrix:

**Likelihood Scale (1–5):**
1. **Rare:** <10% probability of occurrence
2. **Unlikely:** 10–30% probability
3. **Possible:** 30–50% probability
4. **Likely:** 50–70% probability
5. **Almost Certain:** >70% probability

**Consequence/Impact Scale (1–5):**
1. **Negligible:** Minimal impact; <$100K cost impact, <1 week schedule impact

**Risk Score = Likelihood × Consequence** (Range: 1–25)

**Risk Severity Thresholds:**
- **Low Risk (Green):** Score 1–6 → Accept and monitor
- **Medium Risk (Yellow):** Score 7–15 → Mitigation plan required; active management
- **High Risk (Red):** Score 16–25 → Immediate mitigation; executive escalation; may require contract change, alternate sourcing, or design change

### 10.5 Risk Mitigation Strategies by Category

#### **10.5.1 Supplier Performance Risk Mitigation**

**Proactive Measures:**
- **Early Warning System:** Automated monitoring of supplier delivery commitments; alerts triggered when deliveries fall >5 days behind schedule
- **Supplier Scorecards:** Monthly performance review (Section 5.4); Yellow/Red status triggers corrective action escalation
- **Resident Quality Engineers:** Co-located at top 5 Tier 1 suppliers during LRIP to catch quality issues before shipment
- **Contract Incentives:** On-time delivery bonuses (e.g., 2% of contract value for 100% OTD over 12-month period)

**Reactive Measures:**
- **Corrective Action Process:** Formal CAR (Corrective Action Request) for Red-status suppliers; 8D root cause analysis required within 10 days
- **Alternate Sourcing Activation:** Pre-qualified backup suppliers for dual-sourced items; can activate within 60 days
- **Eurus Direct Support:** Technical assistance teams deployed to struggling suppliers (process improvement, quality troubleshooting)
- **Contract Termination:** Reserved right to terminate for cause and re-source if performance does not improve within 90 days of CAR issuance

---

#### **10.5.2 Technical/Design Risk Mitigation**

**Design Maturity:**
- **Phased Design Reviews:** PDR, CDR, and Test Readiness Reviews with independent technical review boards
- **Prototyping and First Articles:** All custom-designed items (transmission, rotor blades, composite structures) require first article testing before production commitment
- **Design Margin Management:** Weight margin (Section 3.7, C-6), performance margin (10% on critical parameters), stress margin (1.5× limit load minimum for primary structure)
- **Modeling & Simulation:** Extensive use of FEA (finite element analysis) for structures, CFD (computational fluid dynamics) for aerodynamics, validated against test data

**Certification Risk:**
- **Early FAA Engagement:** Pre-application meetings; certification basis negotiated at PDR
- **Incremental Compliance:** Rolling compliance demonstrations rather than single end-of-program certification event
- **Government Oversight:** DER (Designated Engineering Representative) involvement in critical design reviews
- **Certification Testing:** Conservative test matrices; multiple test articles to failure to demonstrate margin

---

  - Equipment utilization data (target: <80% utilization to allow surge)

  - Capital investment plans (equipment,

#### **10.5.3 Capacity/Scalability Risk Mitigation**

**Supplier Capacity Assessment (Annual):**
- Conducted by Commodity Managers in collaboration with Manufacturing Engineering
- **Inputs:** 
  - Supplier facility tours and capacity audits
  - Equipment utilization data (target: <80% utilization to allow surge)
  - Workforce demographics (attrition risk, skilled labor availability)
  - Capital investment plans (equipment, facility expansion)
- **Outputs:** 
  - Capacity bottleneck identification
  - Investment recommendations (Eurus-funded or supplier-funded with volume guarantees)
  - Alternate sourcing triggers (if capacity shortfall cannot be closed)

**Capacity Expansion Investment:**
- Total estimated supplier capacity investment required for FRP: $250M+ (external supplier-funded, enabled by Eurus purchase commitments)
- Eurus direct investment: $35M (tooling advances, strategic equipment purchases amortized into unit pricing)
- Investment triggers tied to formal program phase gates (LRIP-2, LRIP-3) to manage financial risk

**Buffer Strategies:**
- **Inventory Buffers:** Strategic buffer stock for long-lead items (Section 8.3.2)
- **Supplier WIP Holdings:** Agreements for suppliers to maintain work-in-progress (WIP) inventory; Eurus takes delivery upon acceptance (reduces Eurus carrying cost)
- **Surge Capacity Contracts:** Pre-negotiated overtime and second-shift rates for critical path suppliers

---

#### **10.5.4 Single-Source/Dependency Risk Mitigation**

**Sole-Source Mitigation Hierarchy:**

**Tier 1: Accept Single-Source with Enhanced Protections**
- **Conditions:** No technical alternative exists; development cost/schedule for second source prohibitive; supplier has strong financial health and track record
- **Protections:**
  - Long-term agreements with delivery guarantees and liquidated damages
  - Escrow of tooling and technical data (enables production transfer if supplier defaults)
  - Financial health monitoring (quarterly D&B reports, annual audited financials review)
  - Business continuity insurance (covers re-procurement costs if supplier fails)
  
**Tier 2: Develop Second Source**
- **Triggers:** Item is critical path, high value (>$1M/aircraft), or supplier financial health questionable
- **Timeline:** Typically 18–36 months for second-source qualification
- **Investment:** $5M–$15M per major item (Eurus-funded NRE for second qualification)
- **Examples:** Engines, landing gear, rotor blades (per Section 7)

**Tier 3: Redesign for Multi-Source**
- **Last Resort:** If single-source risk cannot be mitigated and supplier leverage is unacceptable
- **Example:** Avionics selected for MOSA compliance specifically to avoid vendor lock-in (Section 7.3.1)

---

#### **10.5.5 Geopolitical/External Risk Mitigation**

**Supply Chain Diversification:**
- **Geographic Strategy:** 
  - Tier 1 suppliers: Prefer domestic (US) or NATO/Five-Eyes nations
  - Tier 2/3: Accept international suppliers if dual-sourced or non-critical path
  - **China Exclusion Policy:** No direct China-sourced content for ITAR-controlled aircraft; commercial-off-the-shelf (COTS) electronics screened for China manufacturing origin
- **Conflict Minerals Compliance:** Section 1502 Dodd-Frank Act; suppliers certify conflict-free sourcing (3TG: tin, tantalum, tungsten, gold)

**Trade Compliance:**
- **ITAR/EAR Management:** All suppliers handling controlled technical data registered and compliant (Section 11)
- **Tariff Exposure:** Contracts with international suppliers include tariff escalation clauses; ongoing monitoring of Section 301/232 tariffs
- **Force Majeure Clauses:** Standard contract terms address natural disasters, pandemics, government actions; allocation of risk and relief provisions

**Pandemic/Disruption Preparedness:**
- **Lessons from COVID-19:** Supplier business continuity plans (BCPs) reviewed during qualification
- **Pandemic Response Protocol:** 
  - Tier 1 suppliers required to maintain 30-day safety stock of critical sub-tier components
  - Alternative logistics routes pre-identified (air freight vs. ocean freight vs.
