#### **10.5.6 Technology Obsolescence Risk Mitigation**

**Obsolescence Management Strategy (Electronics Focus):**

**Proactive:**
- **MOSA Architecture:** Open-architecture avionics (Section 7.3.1) designed for modular component replacement without aircraft-level redesign
- **Lifecycle Forecasting:** Electronics suppliers provide 10-year production commitment or technology roadmap; end-of-life (EOL) notifications contractually required 24 months in advance
- **Design for Sustainability:** Where feasible, use long-lifecycle components (industrial/automotive-grade vs. consumer-grade electronics); avoid bleeding-edge technology in baseline design

**Reactive:**
- **Lifetime Buys:** Strategic procurement of long-lead-time ICs and components when EOL announced; quantity sized for remaining production + 10 years spares
  - **Example:** If critical flight computer chip discontinued at LRIP-3, purchase 500-unit lot (covers 230 aircraft production + spares pool)
  - **Cost:** Typically $2M–$8M per lifetime buy; budgeted in program reserves
- **Form/Fit/Function Replacement:** Engineering change to substitute equivalent components; requires qualification testing but avoids full redesign
- **Emulation/FPGA Solutions:** Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) can emulate obsolete ASICs; last-resort solution for critical, irreplaceable components

**Technology Refresh Plan:**
- **Avionics:** Planned 5-year refresh cycle (Section 7.3.1); Gen-2 processors at FRP-1, Gen-3 by sustainment phase
- **Mission Systems:** ISR sensors on 5-year technology insertion cycle (Section 7.3.2)
- **Contract Structure:** Pre-negotiated technology refresh pricing in Tier 1 avionics contracts; supplier provides upgrade path at fixed percentage of original unit cost (e.g., 60% of original price for mid-life upgrade)

---

#### **10.5.7 Cost/Economic Risk Mitigation**

**Material Cost Volatility:**
- **Hedging Strategies:**
  - **Fixed-Price Contracts:** Multi-year agreements lock in pricing for commodity items (aluminum, fasteners)
  - **Economic Price Adjustment (EPA) Clauses:** For long-term contracts, index-based adjustments (Section 9.4.2); protects both parties from extreme volatility
  - **Commodity Futures:** Evaluated for carbon fiber prepreg (high price volatility); pilot program at FRP gate if cost-effective
- **Dual-Source Competition:** Price competition between qualified suppliers (landing gear, actuators, machining); annual re-compete for 20–30% of volume drives cost discipline

**Supplier Financial Health:**
- **Monitoring Frequency:**
  - **Tier 1 Strategic Suppliers:** Quarterly D&B rating checks, annual audited financials review
  - **Tier 2 Preferred Suppliers:** Semi-annual D&B checks
  - **Tier 3 Commodity Suppliers:** Annual review or event-triggered (e.g., bankruptcy news)
- **Red Flag Triggers:**
  - D&B rating drops below 3A2 (or equivalent)
  - Negative equity or going-concern opinion in audited financials
  - Payment default to sub-tier suppliers (detected via industry intelligence)
  - Layoffs or facility closures announced
- **Intervention Actions:**
  - **Green Status:** Continue monitoring
  - **Yellow Status:** Increase payment terms to net-15 (faster cash infusion); activate second source (if exists); request financial recovery plan
  - **Red Status:** Withhold new orders; execute contingency plan (alternate sourcing, production transfer via escrowed tooling);

**Currency Risk (International Suppliers):**
- Contracts denominated in USD where possible
- For Euro/GBP/CAD suppliers: Currency hedging via forward contracts or natural hedging (revenues in same currency offset costs)

---

#### **10.5.8 Quality/Certification Risk Mitigation**

**Quality System Oversight:**
- **Supplier Audits:** 
  - **Tier 1:** Annual AS9100 surveillance audits by Eurus Quality team; coordinate with third-party registrar audits
  - **Tier 2:** Biennial audits or event-driven (quality escape, customer complaint)
  - **Special Process Audits:** NADCAP-certified processes (heat treat, welding, NDT, chemical processing) audited at qualification and every 2 years
- **First Article Inspection (FAI):** Mandatory per AS9102 for all production parts; no lot acceptance without FAI approval (Section 5.3)
- **In-Process Inspection:** Witness/hold points defined in inspection plans for critical characteristics; Eurus Quality Engineer sign-off required before next operation

**Counterfeit Parts Prevention:**
- **AS6081 / AS5553 Compliance:** Suppliers required to source electronic components only from OEMs or authorized distributors; chain of custody documentation
- **Incoming Inspection:** 100% inspection of high-risk items (electronics, raw materials) for authenticity; use of X-ray, decapsulation, and electrical testing for suspect parts
- **Traceability:** Lot traceability to raw material heat lot for all primary structure materials; QR code/serialization for major assemblies

**Non-Conformance Management:**
- **Supplier Notification:** 24-hour notification requirement for non-conforming material discovered at supplier
- **Material Review Board (MRB):** Joint Eurus-supplier MRB for disposition (use-as-is, rework, scrap, return-to-supplier)
- **Root Cause Analysis:** 8D methodology required for all major non-conformances (DPMO >1000, safety impact, schedule impact >1 week)
- **Corrective Action Verification:** Eurus Quality Engineer verifies corrective action implementation before lot release

### 10.6 Supply Chain Risk Monitoring & Reporting

**Risk Dashboard (Monthly Supply Chain Operations Review):**
- Visual risk heat map (5×5 matrix) showing all active risks by likelihood/consequence
- Trend indicators (risk score increasing/decreasing vs. prior month)
- Top 5 risks by score with mitigation status (on track / at risk / off track)
- New risks identified in past 30 days
- Closed risks (mitigated to acceptable level)

**Risk Reporting Cadence:**
- **Weekly:** Critical path risks (score ≥16) briefed to Program Manager
- **Monthly:** Full risk register reviewed at Supply Chain Operations Review (Section 3.3)
- **Quarterly:** Risk trends and aggregate risk score reported to Quarterly Supply Chain Business Review and Program Risk Management Board
- **Event-Driven:** Immediate escalation for new high-risk events (supplier bankruptcy, natural disaster impacting supplier, cyber attack, quality escape)

**Risk Metrics:**
- **Aggregate Risk Score:** Sum of (Likelihood × Consequence) for all open risks; target: reduce by 60% from baseline (CDR) to FRP gate
- **Risk Closure Rate:** Number of risks closed per quarter vs. new risks opened; target: net reduction after LRIP-3
- **Mitigation Effectiveness:** Percentage of mitigations executed on time and achieving intended risk reduction; target: ≥85%

## 11. EXPORT CONTROL & TRUSTED SUPPLIER REQUIREMENTS

### 11.1 Regulatory Framework

The MAAP-1 program involves technologies and technical data controlled under:
- **International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR):** 22 CFR §120-130, administered by U.S. Department of State, Directorate of Defense Trade Controls (DDTC)
  - **USML Category VIII(h):** Military aircraft (rotorcraft exceeding specified performance)
  - **USML Category XI(a):** Military electronics (fire control, autonomous navigation)
- **Export Administration Regulations (EAR):** 15 CFR §730-774, administered by U.S. Department of Commerce, Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS)
  - **ECCN 9A610:** Military aircraft not controlled under USML Category VIII (export variants with reduced capability)
  - **ECCN 3A001/4A003:** Certain avionics and electronics subject to dual-use controls

**Eurus Systems ITAR Registration:** K-12345 (current through December 2026)

---

### 11.2 Technology Control Plan (TCP)

A comprehensive Technology Control Plan (TCP-MAAP1-001, current revision C) governs all export-controlled aspects of the MAAP-1 program. The TCP is reviewed and updated at each program phase gate and approved by the Export Compliance Manager and Program Manager.

**TCP Key Elements:**
1. **USML Categorization:** All MAAP-1 hardware, software, and technical data classified by USML/ECCN category
2. **Supplier Registration Verification:** All suppliers handling ITAR-controlled technical data verified for current DDTC registration
3. **Foreign Person Access Controls:** Procedures for Technology Control Plans (TCPs) when foreign persons (including permanent residents) access controlled data
4. **Export Licensing Strategy:** Pre-determined licensing approach for anticipated FMS customers (NATO, Five-Eyes, Major Non-NATO Allies)
5. **Technical Data Segregation:** Architecture for separating ITAR-controlled design data from exportable data (enables coalition sales without full technical data release)

---

### 11.3 Supplier Export Compliance Requirements

#### **11.3.1 ITAR-Registered Suppliers (Tier 1 & Select Tier 2)**

**Mandatory Requirements:**
- **DDTC Registration:** Current and valid ITAR registration; Eurus verifies via DDTC public portal quarterly
- **Technology Control Plan (Supplier TCP):** Suppliers receiving ITAR technical data must maintain an approved TCP; Eurus audits during qualification and annually thereafter
- **Foreign National Access Controls:** Suppliers must screen employees for U.S. Person status before granting access to ITAR data; foreign persons require export license (typically DSP-5 for permanent employment)
- **Secure Data Handling:** 
  - Technical data marked with ITAR distribution statement (e.g., "Export Controlled — ITAR 22 CFR §120-130")
  - Electronic data transmitted via encrypted channels (minimum AES-256)
  - Physical data stored in controlled access areas (badge access, visitor logs)
- **Re-Export Restrictions:** Suppliers prohibited from re-exporting ITAR-controlled items or data without Eurus and DDTC authorization
- **Audit Rights:** Eurus and U.S.

- **Audit Rights:** Eurus and U.S. Government (DDTC, DCSA) reserve right to audit supplier export compliance program

**Supplier Training:**
- ITAR awareness training provided by Eurus at contract kickoff (web-based module, 90 minutes)
- Annual refresher training required
- Supplier compliance officer designated as point of contact for export matters

---

#### **11.3.2 Non-ITAR Suppliers (Tier 3 & Commodity)**

**Requirements:**
- **EAR Compliance:** If supplier receives EAR-controlled technical data (e.g., drawings for commercial-grade components), must comply with EAR restrictions (no export to embargoed countries, sanctioned parties)
- **Deemed Export Awareness:** Suppliers made aware that sharing technical data with foreign nationals may constitute a "deemed export" requiring authorization
- **Screening Against Denied Parties Lists:** Eurus screens all suppliers (ITAR and non-ITAR) against:
  - DDTC Debarred List
  - BIS Denied Persons List / Entity List
  - OFAC Specially Designated Nationals (SDN) List
  - Consolidated Screening List
  - **Frequency:** At contract award and quarterly thereafter

---

### 11.4 Export Variant Strategy

To enable international sales without full technical data release and to accommodate customers without access to U.S.-controlled technologies, the program includes defined **export variants**:

#### **11.4.1 MAAP-1C Export (Cargo Variant)**

**Baseline vs. Export Differences:**

| System | Domestic Baseline | Export Variant | USML/ECCN Impact |
|--------|------------------|----------------|------------------|
| **Autonomous GNC** | Level 3 autonomy (supervised autonomous) | Level 2 (optionally-piloted only); autonomous functions disabled or removed | Reduces USML XI(a) sensitivity; shifts some functionality to ECCN 7D003 (navigation software) |
| **GPS/INS** | M-code military GPS receiver | Commercial P(Y)-code GPS | Removes USML restriction; becomes ECCN 7A003 |
| **Secure Communications** | Type-1 NSA-certified encryption | Commercial encryption (AES-256, non-Type-1) | Removes USML restriction; becomes EAR99 or ECCN 5A002 |
| **Datalink** | Link 16 (MIDS-JTRS) | Omitted or replaced with commercial SATCOM-only | Removes USML XI(c) tactical datalink restriction |
| **Defensive Systems** | Missile warning sensors | Omitted (not included in Cargo baseline; customer option) | N/A for baseline export Cargo variant |
| **Engine** | Domestic turboshaft (GE T700 or equiv) | FAA-certified commercial derivative (e.g., GE CT7) with equivalent power | Removes USML VIII(h) military-specific engine restriction |

**Export License Requirement:**
- **NATO / Five-Eyes / Major Non-NATO Allies (MNNA):** Covered under pre-approved export authorization (e.g., DSP-5 Technical Assistance Agreement or FMS LOA); no case-by-case license for routine sales
- **Other Countries:** Case-by-case export license (DSP-5 or DSP-6) required; approved based on U.S. foreign policy considerations

**Technical Data Release:**
- **Operational/Maintenance Data:** Released to export customers (flight manuals, maintenance manuals, illustrated parts catalogs)
- **Manufacturing/Design Data:** Withheld or released under restrictive Technical Assistance Agreement (TAA); prevents customer from reverse-engineering or producing spare parts domestically without U.S.

foreign policy considerations

- **Operational/Maintenance Data:** Released to export customers (flight manuals, maintenance manuals, illustrated parts catalogs)
- **Manufacturing/Design Data:** Withheld or released under restrictive Technical Assistance Agreement (TAA); prevents customer from reverse-engineering or producing spare parts domestically without U.S. authorization

---

#### **11.4.2 MAAP-1I Export (ISR Variant)**

**Challenge:** ISR sensors (EO/IR, SAR, SIGINT) are highly export-sensitive (USML Category XI)

**Export Strategy:**
- **Tiered Sensor Packages:**
  - **Tier 1 (Five-Eyes + Select Allies):** Full-capability sensors (subject to case-by-case approval and end-use monitoring)
  - **Tier 2 (NATO / MNNA):** Reduced-capability sensors (lower resolution EO/IR, no SIGINT) or commercial equivalents
  - **Tier 3 (Other):** Commercial-off-the-shelf sensors only; no military-specific mission management software
- **Modular Architecture:** Sensor bay designed per ICD-003 (Section 5.3) to accommodate customer-furnished sensors; enables customer to integrate locally-sourced ISR equipment

---

### 11.5 Foreign Military Sales (FMS) Strategy

**Anticipated Customer Base:**
- **Primary:** NATO allies (identified interest from [Country A], [Country B] — specific names export controlled per TCP)
- **Secondary:** Five-Eyes partners (Australia, Canada) for special operations / disaster response variants
- **Tertiary:** Major Non-NATO Allies (MNNA) in Middle East and Asia-Pacific

**FMS Process Integration:**
- **Letter of Request (LOR) Phase:** Eurus supports U.S. Government in developing Price & Availability (P&A) data; export variant specifications provided
- **Letter of Offer and Acceptance (LOA) Phase:** Eurus designated as prime contractor for FMS deliveries; contracts with DCMA as FMS Administrative Contracting Officer (ACO)
- **Delivery Timeline:** FMS aircraft typically delivered 12–18 months after domestic deliveries (due to export license processing and configuration differences)
- **Offsets / Industrial Participation:** Evaluated case-by-case based on customer requirements; potential for licensed production of sub-assemblies (e.g., wiring harnesses, secondary structures) in customer nation (requires separate TAA and manufacturing license)

### 11.6 Cybersecurity & Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI)

**NIST SP 800-171 Compliance:**
- All suppliers handling **Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI)** — which includes ITAR-controlled technical data, export-controlled data, and For Official Use Only (FOUO) program data — must comply with NIST SP 800-171 (Protecting Controlled Unclassified Information in Nonfederal Systems and Organizations).

**Supplier Assessment Process:**
1. **Self-Assessment:** Supplier completes NIST SP 800-171 self-assessment (110 security controls across 14 families)
2. **Eurus Review:** Export Compliance Manager and IT Security review self-assessment; identify gaps
3. **System Security Plan (SSP):** Suppliers with gaps must provide SSP documenting compensating controls or remediation timeline
4. **Audit:** High-risk suppliers (Tier 1, handling classified or sensitive CUI) subject to on-site cybersecurity audit by Eurus IT Security team or third-party assessor
5.

**Supplier Assessment Process:**
1. **Self-Assessment:** Supplier completes NIST SP 800-171 self-assessment (110 security controls across 14 families)
2. **Eurus Review:** Export Compliance Manager and IT Security review self-assessment; identify gaps
3. **System Security Plan (SSP):** Suppliers with gaps must provide SSP documenting compensating controls or remediation timeline
4. **Audit:** High-risk suppliers (Tier 1, handling classified or sensitive CUI) subject to on-site cybersecurity audit by Eurus IT Security team or third-party assessor
5. **Certification:** Supplier certifies compliance annually; material breach of cybersecurity requirements is grounds for contract termination

**CMMC Preparation (Future):**
- DoD Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) will eventually replace NIST SP 800-171 self-assessment with third-party certification
- Eurus is monitoring CMMC rulemaking; suppliers will be required to achieve CMMC Level 2 (or successor) prior to FRP gate
- Eurus investment: $500K program budget for supplier CMMC readiness support (gap assessments, remediation funding for strategic suppliers)

---

### 11.7 Export Compliance Audits & Enforcement

**Internal Audits:**
- Export Compliance Manager conducts annual internal audits of Eurus export controls program; findings reported to Executive Leadership
- Supplier export compliance audits (Section 11.3.1) conducted at supplier facilities; typically 1-day audit covering TCP implementation, data handling, foreign person access logs

**Government Audits:**
- **DDTC Compliance Reviews:** DDTC may conduct announced or unannounced audits of ITAR registrants; Eurus last audited [Date TBD]; no significant findings
- **DCSA (Defense Counterintelligence and Security Agency):** For classified programs (MAAP-1 baseline is unclassified; ISR variant may involve classified annexes), DCSA conducts facility security clearance reviews
- **Blue Lantern End-Use Monitoring:** For FMS cases, DDTC/State Department conducts post-delivery inspections (Blue Lantern checks) to verify end-use and prevent diversion; Eurus coordinates with customers to facilitate

**Enforcement & Penalties:**
- **Voluntary Disclosure:** If Eurus discovers a potential export violation (e.g., inadvertent release of ITAR data to unauthorized party), immediate voluntary disclosure to DDTC per 22 CFR §127.12; mitigates penalties
- **Penalties for Violations:** ITAR violations can result in:
  - Civil penalties: up to $1,191,780 per violation (adjusted annually for inflation)
  - Criminal penalties: up to $1,000,000 fine and 20 years imprisonment (per 22 USC §2778)
  - Debarment: Loss of export privileges
- **Supplier Violations:** Suppliers in violation of ITAR/EAR reported to Eurus immediately; Eurus withholds further controlled data and may terminate contract; Eurus liability for supplier violations avoided through robust supplier vetting and audit program

## 12. ALIGNMENT WITH MANUFACTURING RAMP & IMS

### 12.1 Integration with Integrated Master Schedule (IMS)

The supply chain procurement schedule is fully integrated into the Program Integrated Master Schedule (IMS-0001, current revision) maintained in Microsoft Project (or equivalent scheduling tool). All supplier delivery commitments are captured as discrete schedule activities with assigned durations, predecessors/successors, and resource loading.

ALIGNMENT WITH MANUFACTURING RAMP & IMS

### 12.1 Integration with Integrated Master Schedule (IMS)

The supply chain procurement schedule is fully integrated into the Program Integrated Master Schedule (IMS-0001, current revision) maintained in Microsoft Project (or equivalent scheduling tool). All supplier delivery commitments are captured as discrete schedule activities with assigned durations, predecessors/successors, and resource loading.

**IMS Hierarchy:**
- **Level 1:** Program Milestones (e.g., CDR, First Flight, LRIP Decision, IOC)
- **Level 2:** Major Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) elements (e.g., WBS 3.2 Manufacturing, WBS 3.3 Supply Chain, WBS 4.1 Test)
- **Level 3:** Subsystem procurement and manufacturing flows (e.g., WBS 3.3.1 Propulsion Procurement, WBS 3.2.1 Final Assembly)
- **Level 4:** Individual purchase orders, supplier deliveries, and manufacturing operations (e.g., PO-12345 Engine Delivery Aircraft #1)

**Supply Chain Critical Path:**
- As of current IMS baseline (January 2025), supply chain activities are on the program critical path through aircraft #22 (LRIP-3)
- **Critical Path Items:** Engines, main transmission, landing gear forgings, composite fuselage barrels (see Section 8.2)
- **Float:** Supply chain float increases from zero (aircraft #1) to 3 weeks (aircraft #22) as manufacturing cycle time becomes dominant constraint
- **Critical Path Transition:** By FRP-1 (aircraft #46), final assembly and flight test become critical path; supply chain provides 4–6 weeks float (assuming no supplier disruptions)

**IMS Maintenance:**
- **Monthly Updates:** Buyers update PO delivery dates in ERP; ERP interfaces with IMS to update supplier delivery activities automatically
- **Weekly Critical Path Review:** Program Scheduler and SC Director review critical path activities; expedite actions initiated for any activity with <2 weeks float
- **Quarterly Baseline Updates:** IMS re-baselined quarterly after Program Control Board review; incorporates approved schedule changes, engineering changes, and supplier delivery adjustments

---

### 12.2 Material Requirements Planning (MRP) Integration

**ERP System:** Eurus Systems operates an integrated ERP system (assumed: SAP S/4HANA, Oracle ERP Cloud, or equivalent) with full MRP II capabilities.

**MRP Process Flow:**

```
Master Production Schedule (MPS)
  ↓
Bill of Materials (BOM) Explosion
  ↓
Inventory On-Hand / On-Order Check
  ↓
Net Requirements Calculation
  ↓
Lead Time Offsetting
  ↓
Purchase Requisition Generation
  ↓
Buyer Review & PO Release
  ↓
Supplier Acknowledgment & Delivery Tracking
  ↓
Receiving & Inspection
  ↓
Inventory Available for Production
```

**MRP Parameters by Item Category:**

| Category | Lead Time | Order Policy | Safety Stock | Lot Size |
|----------|-----------|--------------|--------------|----------|
| **Engines** | 52 weeks | Discrete (per aircraft tail number) | 2 units (test + spare) | 1 (dedicated to aircraft) |
| **Transmissions** | 40 weeks | Discrete | 1 unit | 1 |
| **Landing Gear** | 24 weeks | Lot-for-Lot (batch of 6) | Zero (supplier holds WIP) | 6 |
| **Composite Structures** | 22 weeks | Lot-for-Lot (batch of 4) | Zero | 4 |
| **Avionics LRUs** | 20 weeks | Batch (12 units) | 10% of annual demand | 12 |
| **Machined Components** | 10–14 weeks | Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) | 4 weeks supply | EOQ formula |
| **Fasteners / Hardware** | 8 weeks | Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI) | 30 days (supplier-held) | Replenishment per VMI agreement |
| **Raw Materials (aluminum, prepreg)** | 6–8 weeks | EOQ or Blanket PO | 6 weeks supply | EOQ or monthly call-off |

**MRP Run Frequency:**
- **Weekly MRP Run:** Full requirements regeneration every Monday; updated purchase requisitions generated for buyer review
- **Daily Net Change MRP:** Incremental updates for engineering changes, demand changes, or receipt variances; triggers exception messages for buyer action

**MRP Exception Management:**
- **Exceptions Monitored:**
  - **Past Due:** PO delivery date is in the past; expedite action required
  - **Late:** PO delivery date will not meet production need date; expedite or reschedule production
  - **Excess Inventory:** On-hand inventory exceeds demand forecast by >30 days; consider canceling or postponing open POs
  - **Shortage:** Net requirement cannot be met by current POs; emergency procurement required
- **Daily Exception Report:** Auto-generated and emailed to buyers and manufacturing planners; reviewed at Weekly Supply Chain Operations Review

---

### 12.3 Procurement Lead Time vs.

Manufacturing Cycle Time

**Manufacturing Cycle Time (Final Assembly):**
- **LRIP-1 (Aircraft #1–#4):** 180 days (6 months) from first structural assembly to flight test delivery
  - Learning curve steep; first article quality issues; limited tooling/workforce
- **LRIP-2 (Aircraft #5–#10):** 120 days (4 months)
  - Process stabilization; workforce learning; tooling optimization
- **LRIP-3 (Aircraft #11–#22):** 75 days (2.5 months)
  - Flow-line assembly begins; improved productivity
- **FRP-1 (Aircraft #23–#46):** 45 days (1.5 months)
  - Full flow-line; 7 assembly positions; throughput-optimized
- **FRP-2 (Aircraft #47+):** 30 days (1 month) at peak rate (48 aircraft/year = 1 aircraft every 7.5 days entering final assembly; 30-day flow time)

**Implication for Procurement:**
- Long-lead items (engines 52 weeks, transmission 40 weeks) must be ordered well before manufacturing cycle time
- **LRIP-1:** Order engines 12 months before manufacturing start (18 months before flight test delivery)
- **FRP-2:** Order engines 12 months before assembly start (13 months before delivery); manufacturing cycle time is now only 1 month

**Procurement-to-Manufacturing Handoff:**
- **Kitting Strategy:** All material for an aircraft "kit" staged in manufacturing before assembly start
  - **Kit Complete Date:** Target 2 weeks before assembly start
  - **Kit Shortage Meeting:** Weekly review of upcoming aircraft kits; identify shortages and expedite actions
- **Phased Deliveries:** For large assemblies (fuselage sections), deliveries sequenced to match assembly flow
  - Example: Forward fuselage delivered Week 1, center fuselage Week 3, aft fuselage Week 4 of a 12-week assembly flow

---

### 12.4 Manufacturing Ramp Risk & Supply Chain Flexibility

**Ramp Risk:** The 24× production increase from LRIP-1 (2 aircraft/year) to FRP-2 (48 aircraft/year) over 5 years represents significant risk to both manufacturing and supply chain.

**Supply Chain Flexibility Mechanisms:**

**1. Supplier Capacity Ramp Gates:**
- Formal supplier capacity reviews at each program phase gate (LRIP-2, LRIP-3, FRP-1)
- **Go/No-Go Decision:** Supplier must demonstrate capacity readiness (equipment, workforce, cash flow) before Eurus authorizes production rate increase
- If supplier not ready: Hold rate increase, activate alternate source, or provide Eurus-funded capacity support

**2. Rate-Reduction Options:**
- Contracts include provisions for Eurus to reduce order quantities by up to 25% without penalty (to accommodate program delays or demand changes)
- Supplier receives termination liability for unrecoverable costs (tooling, long-lead materials ordered at Eurus direction)

**3. Rate-Increase Options:**
- Contracts include options for Eurus to increase quantities by up to 50% with 12-month notice
- Pricing for optional quantities negotiated upfront; supplier commits to capacity expansion plan if options exercised

**4. Flexible Delivery Scheduling:**
- Eurus provides 18-month firm forecast, 36-month planning forecast to suppliers
- Firm window: Supplier must deliver per committed dates (liquidated damages apply)
- Planning window: Forecast only; adjustments allowed with 12-month notice

### 13.3 Continuous Improvement Programs

#### **13.3.1 Supplier Cost Reduction Initiatives**

**Target:** 3–5% annual cost reduction from LRIP-2 through FRP-2 (per Section 2.2, Strategic Objective SO-3)

**Mechanisms:**

**Value Engineering (VE) Workshops:**
- Conducted annually with strategic suppliers (Tier 1)
- Cross-functional teams (Eurus Engineering + Manufacturing + Supply Chain; Supplier Engineering + Operations)
- Focus areas:
  - Design simplification (reduce part count, eliminate non-value-added features)
  - Material substitution (e.g., aluminum-lithium for weight savings + cost reduction)
  - Process optimization (automation, cycle time reduction, yield improvement)
- **Example:** Composite supplier VE workshop identifies opportunity to combine two fuselage panels into single co-cured assembly → eliminates 24 fasteners, reduces assembly labor 3 hours → $12K savings per aircraft → $2.76M over 230 aircraft

**Should-Cost Analysis:**
- Eurus Supply Chain and Manufacturing Engineering develop independent should-cost models for high-value items
- Compare supplier pricing to should-cost; identify cost drivers and opportunities
- Collaborative negotiations with supplier to close gaps
- **Tool:** Parametric cost models (cost estimating relationships based on part complexity, material, processes)

**Competitive Re-Sourcing:**
- Non-strategic items (Tier 2/3) re-competed annually or bi-annually
- 20–30% of portfolio re-bid per year to maintain competitive pressure
- Incumbent supplier given opportunity to match or beat competitive pricing

**Learning Curve Management:**
- Track actual supplier labor hours vs.

negotiated learning curve (typically 85–92% slope)
- If supplier outperforms learning curve (due to automation, process improvement), negotiate accelerated cost reduction
- If supplier underperforms, trigger root cause analysis and corrective action

**Annual Savings Target:**
- **LRIP Phase:** $500K–$1M annual savings (primarily VE and should-cost negotiations)
- **FRP Phase:** $3M–$5M annual savings (volume leverage, competitive re-sourcing, learning curve)
- **Cumulative Savings Goal:** $25M over program lifecycle (contributes to 39% unit cost reduction LRIP-1 to FRP-2)

---

#### **13.3.2 Supplier Quality Improvement Programs**

**Target:** Reduce supplier DPMO from <1,000 (LRIP) to <300 (FRP) — see Section 2.2

**Initiatives:**

**Six Sigma Training:**
- Eurus offers Six Sigma Green Belt training to supplier quality and operations personnel (2-week course, online + in-person)
- Goal: Train 50 supplier employees over program lifecycle
- Graduates lead Six Sigma projects at supplier facilities (DMAIC methodology: Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control)

**Statistical Process Control (SPC) Implementation:**
- Critical processes (machining, composite cure, welding) placed under SPC
- Suppliers use control charts (X-bar, R charts) to monitor process variation
- Out-of-control conditions trigger immediate investigation and corrective action before non-conforming parts produced

**First Article Inspection (FAI) Lessons Learned:**
- Post-FAI debriefs capture common issues (tolerance misinterpretation, inadequate tooling, inspection equipment calibration)
- Lessons learned shared across supplier base via quarterly "Supplier Quality Forum"

**Zero-Defect Culture:**
- Supplier quality managers challenged to achieve zero defects delivered to Eurus (100% conformance)
- Recognition program: Suppliers achieving zero defects for 12 consecutive months receive "Supplier Excellence Award" (plaque + public recognition + preferred status for future bids)

---

#### **13.3.3 Delivery Performance Improvement**

**Target:** ≥98% OTD at FRP (Section 2.2)

**Initiatives:**

**Supplier Delivery Visibility:**
- Suppliers required to update delivery status in Eurus supplier portal weekly
- Traffic-light system: Green (on track), Yellow (at risk, mitigation plan required), Red (will be late, expedite action required)

**Root Cause Analysis for Late Deliveries:**
- Every late delivery (>2 days past committed date) triggers root cause analysis (5-Why or Fishbone diagram)
- Common root causes identified and addressed systematically:
  - **Inaccurate Lead Times:** Re-baseline lead times in ERP
  - **Sub-Tier Supplier Issues:** Eurus assists supplier in managing sub-tier (e.g., joint audits, alternate sourcing)
  - **Capacity Constraints:** Trigger capacity expansion discussion (Section 6.3)

**Delivery Performance Incentives:**
- Bonus payments for exceptional OTD performance (e.g., 2% of annual contract value for 100% OTD over 12 months)
- Conversely, liquidated damages for chronic late deliveries (Section 9.3.3)

## 14. SUPPLY CHAIN TECHNOLOGY & DIGITAL INTEGRATION

### 14.1 Digital Supply Chain Vision

Eurus Systems is committed to leveraging Industry 4.0 technologies to achieve a **transparent, predictive, and resilient supply chain** by the FRP-2 phase (2030).

SUPPLY CHAIN TECHNOLOGY & DIGITAL INTEGRATION

### 14.1 Digital Supply Chain Vision

Eurus Systems is committed to leveraging Industry 4.0 technologies to achieve a **transparent, predictive, and resilient supply chain** by the FRP-2 phase (2030). The vision includes:

- **End-to-end visibility:** Real-time tracking of materials from raw material supplier → Tier 2 → Tier 1 → Eurus → final assembly
- **Predictive analytics:** AI/ML models forecasting supplier delivery risks, quality issues, and demand changes
- **Digital twin:** Virtual representation of the supply chain enabling scenario planning and risk simulation
- **Automated procurement:** Robotic Process Automation (RPA) for routine PO generation, acknowledgment tracking, and invoice processing

### 14.4 Data Integration & Interoperability

**Challenge:** Supply chain data resides in multiple systems (ERP, PLM, QMS, IMS, Supplier Portal); siloed data hinders visibility and decision-making.

**Solution: Data Integration Platform (APIs + Data Lake)**

**Architecture:**
- **Master Data Management (MDM):** Single source of truth for supplier data (contact info, capabilities, certifications), part data (BOM, specifications), PO data
- **API Layer:** RESTful APIs enable real-time data exchange between ERP, PLM, QMS, IMS
  - Example: Engineering change order (ECO) in PLM automatically triggers BOM update in ERP → MRP recalculation → new purchase requisitions → buyer notification
- **Data Lake:** Centralized repository for historical transactional data (PO history, delivery performance, quality data, cost data)
  - Used for advanced analytics, reporting, and machine learning model training
- **Dashboards & Reporting:** Power BI or Tableau dashboards provide real-time visibility into supply chain KPIs (OTD, DPMO, past-due POs, inventory levels, cost variance)

**Data Governance:**
- Supply Chain Director owns supply chain data governance policy
- Data quality KPIs: ≥98% accuracy for master supplier data, BOM data; monitored monthly
- Annual data audits to identify and remediate data quality issues

---

### 14.5 Cybersecurity for Supply Chain Systems

**Threat Landscape:**
- Supply chain systems are high-value targets for cyberattacks (ransomware, data theft, IP exfiltration)
- Supplier portals are externally accessible → attack surface

**Security Measures:**

**1. Network Segmentation:**
- Supply chain systems (ERP, supplier portal) on separate network segment from engineering systems (PLM, design servers)
- Firewalls and intrusion detection/prevention systems (IDS/IPS) monitor traffic

**2. Access Controls:**
- Multi-factor authentication (MFA) required for all supplier portal access
- Role-based access control (RBAC) in ERP: buyers can create POs, cannot approve; managers approve POs, cannot edit master data
- Privileged access management (PAM) for system administrators

**3. Data Encryption:**
- Data at rest: AES-256 encryption for databases (ERP, QMS, supplier portal)
- Data in transit: TLS 1.2+ for all web traffic; VPN for supplier file transfers (technical drawings, test data)

**4.

- Supplier cyber incidents reported to Eurus within 72 hours per contract terms

## 15. QUALITY ASSURANCE & SUPPLIER OVERSIGHT

### 15.1 Quality Management System (QMS) Overview

Eurus Systems Quality Assurance organization operates an AS9100D-certified QMS covering all aspects of design, manufacturing, and supply chain management. The QMS is integrated with the supply chain procurement process to ensure all purchased materials, components, and services meet requirements.

**Quality Policy (Excerpt):**
> "Eurus Systems is committed to delivering aerospace products and services that meet or exceed customer expectations and regulatory requirements. We achieve this through a culture of continuous improvement, adherence to AS9100 and ISO 9001 standards, rigorous supplier management, and a zero-defect mindset."

---

### 15.2 Supplier Quality Requirements

#### **15.2.1 Mandatory Quality Certifications**

| Supplier Tier | Minimum Certification | Preferred Certification | Special Process Requirements |
|---------------|----------------------|-------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Tier 1 (Strategic)** | AS9100D (current revision) | AS9100 + NADCAP for applicable special processes | NADCAP required for heat treat, welding, NDT, chemical processing, composites (if supplier performs in-house) |
| **Tier 2 (Preferred)** | ISO 9001:2015 | AS9100D | NADCAP if special process critical to aircraft safety |
| **Tier 3 (Commodity)** | ISO 9001:2015 or equivalent | — | N/A for COTS items; special process suppliers must have industry-recognized cert (e.g., AMS, ASTM compliance) |

**Certification Verification:**
- Suppliers provide copy of current certificate during qualification (Section 5.3)
- Eurus Quality audits verify certificate validity and QMS effectiveness (annual surveillance for Tier 1, biennial for Tier 2)

---

#### **15.2.2 First Article Inspection (FAI)**

**Requirement:** All production parts require FAI per AS9102 before lot acceptance (applies to Tier 1, Tier 2, and critical Tier 3 items).

**FAI Process:**
1. **Supplier Preparation:** Supplier manufactures first article using production tooling, processes, and personnel
2. **Full Dimensional Inspection:** Supplier inspects 100% of drawing characteristics; records results on AS9102 Form 3
3. **Material Certification:** Supplier provides material certs (chemical composition, mechanical properties, heat lot traceability)
4. **Special Process Certification:** Supplier provides evidence of special process compliance (heat treat charts, weld procedure qualification records, NDT reports)
5. **FAI Package Submission:** Supplier submits complete FAI package (Form 1: Index, Form 2: Product Accountability, Form 3: Characteristics Accountability, supporting data) to Eurus Quality
6. **Eurus Review:** Supplier Quality Engineer (SQE) reviews FAI package for completeness and compliance; may witness inspection at supplier facility (required for critical/complex parts)
7. **FAI Approval:** SQE approves FAI; part released for production lot acceptance
8.

**Full Dimensional Inspection:** Supplier inspects 100% of drawing characteristics; records results on AS9102 Form 3
3. **Material Certification:** Supplier provides material certs (chemical composition, mechanical properties, heat lot traceability)
4. **Special Process Certification:** Supplier provides evidence of special process compliance (heat treat charts, weld procedure qualification records, NDT reports)
5. **FAI Package Submission:** Supplier submits complete FAI package (Form 1: Index, Form 2: Product Accountability, Form 3: Characteristics Accountability, supporting data) to Eurus Quality
6. **Eurus Review:** Supplier Quality Engineer (SQE) reviews FAI package for completeness and compliance; may witness inspection at supplier facility (required for critical/complex parts)
7. **FAI Approval:** SQE approves FAI; part released for production lot acceptance
8. **FAI Database:** Approved FAI packages stored in QMS database; accessible for future reference (e.g., if supplier changes process, new FAI required)

**Timeline:** FAI review and approval target: 10 business days from submission (critical path items expedited to 5 days)

---

#### **15.2.3 Receiving Inspection**

**Scope:** All incoming purchased materials and components subject to receiving inspection prior to acceptance and use in production.

**FAI Package Submission:** Supplier submits complete FAI package (Form 1: Index, Form 2: Product Accountability, Form 3: Characteristics Accountability, supporting data) to Eurus Quality
6. **Eurus Review:** Supplier Quality Engineer (SQE) reviews FAI package for completeness and compliance; may witness inspection at supplier facility (required for critical/complex parts)
7. **FAI Approval:** SQE approves FAI; part released for production lot acceptance
8. **FAI Database:** Approved FAI packages stored in QMS database; accessible for future reference (e.g., if supplier changes process, new FAI required)

**Timeline:** FAI review and approval target: 10 business days from submission (critical path items expedited to 5 days)

---

#### **15.2.3 Receiving Inspection**

**Scope:** All incoming purchased materials and components subject to receiving inspection prior to acceptance and use in production.

**Inspection Levels:**

| Level | Description | When Applied |
|-------|-------------|--------------|
| **Level 1: Documentation Review** | Review packing slip, material certs, CoC (Certificate of Conformance); no physical inspection | COTS items from established suppliers with good quality history (Green scorecard status) |
| **Level 2: Sample Inspection** | Visual inspection + dimensional check of sample (10% of lot or 3 pieces, whichever greater) | Standard production parts from qualified suppliers |
| **Level 3: 100% Inspection** | Full dimensional inspection of every piece in lot | First article, critical safety items, new suppliers, suppliers with quality history (Yellow/Red scorecard) |
| **Level 4: Source Inspection** | Eurus SQE inspects at supplier facility before shipment (Eurus acceptance at source) | Long-lead, high-value, complex assemblies (engines, transmissions, landing gear) |

**Non-Conforming Material:**
- Material failing receiving inspection quarantined (red-tagged, segregated area)
- Material Review Board (MRB) dispositions non-conformance: Use-As-Is, Rework, Return-to-Supplier, Scrap
- Supplier issued Supplier Corrective Action Request (SCAR); root cause analysis and corrective action required (8D report within 10 business days)

**Inspection Equipment:**
- Coordinate Measuring Machines (CMM) for complex geometry
- Optical comparators, micrometers, calipers for standard dimensions
- Material test lab (tensile testing, hardness testing, chemical analysis) for material verification
- Eurus-owned equipment maintained per ISO 10012 (calibration every 6–12 months; NIST-traceable)

---

### 15.3 Supplier Audits

**Audit Types:**

#### **15.3.1 New Supplier Qualification Audit**
- **Timing:** Prior to supplier selection (Section 5.2, Phase 2)
- **Scope:** Full QMS audit (AS9100 compliance); facility tour; interviews with quality, engineering, operations personnel; review of past performance data
- **Duration:** 1–2 days on-site
- **Team:** Lead Auditor (Eurus Quality Manager or senior SQE) + Commodity Manager + Engineering representative (if technical assessment needed)
- **Output:** Audit report with findings (major non-conformances, minor non-conformances, observations); supplier must close major findings before contract award

#### **15.3.2 Surveillance Audit (Annual)**
- **Timing:** Annual for Tier 1 Strategic Suppliers; biennial for Tier 2 Preferred Suppliers
- **Scope:** Focused audit on key processes (receiving inspection, production control, calibration, training, corrective action system); review of supplier performance metrics; verify closure of prior audit findings
- **Duration:** 1 day on-site
- **Output:** Updated audit report; findings tracked in QMS

#### **15.3.3 For-Cause Audit**
- **Trigger:** Significant quality issue (e.g., multiple non-conformances, safety-related defect, customer complaint), supplier scorecard Red status for 2+ consecutive months, or risk event (financial distress, management change, facility damage)
- **Timing:** Within 30 days of trigger event
- **Scope:** Deep-dive root cause investigation; may include sub-tier supplier audits if issue originates there
- **Output:** Comprehensive corrective action plan; may result in supplier probation (no new orders until corrective action verified) or disqualification

**Special Process Audits (NADCAP):**
- For suppliers performing NADCAP-accredited special processes (heat treat, welding, NDT, chemical processing, composites), Eurus coordinates audit with PRI (Performance Review Institute, NADCAP administrator)
- Eurus SQE may participate in NADCAP audit as observer
- Suppliers must maintain current NADCAP accreditation; lapse results in suspension of special process work until re-accreditation

---

### 15.4 Corrective Action & Continuous Improvement (CAPA)

**Supplier Corrective Action Request (SCAR) Process:**

**Trigger:** Non-conforming material, delivery issue, quality system deficiency identified during audit, or customer complaint

**SCAR Workflow:**
1.

**SCAR Issuance:** Eurus Quality issues SCAR to supplier via QMS portal; includes description of issue, supporting evidence (photos, inspection reports, customer complaint), required response date (typically 10 business days for root cause, 30 days for corrective action implementation)
2. **Supplier Acknowledgment:** Supplier acknowledges SCAR receipt within 24 hours
3. **Root Cause Analysis:** Supplier conducts root cause analysis using 8D (Eight Disciplines) or equivalent methodology:
   - **D1:** Form team
   - **D2:** Describe problem
   - **D3:** Implement containment actions (prevent further non-conforming material from shipping)
   - **D4:** Identify root cause (5-Why, Fishbone, Fault Tree Analysis)
   - **D5:** Choose corrective actions
   - **D6:** Implement corrective actions
   - **D7:** Prevent recurrence (update procedures, training, process controls)
   - **D8:** Congratulate team
4. **Eurus Review:** SQE reviews 8D report; approves corrective action plan or requests additional information/clarification
5. **Verification:** SQE verifies corrective action effectiveness (may require on-site verification visit, review of inspection data, or submission of revised FAI)
6. **SCAR Closure:** Once verified effective, SCAR closed in QMS; remains in supplier's quality history for future reference

**SCAR Escalation:**
- **Minor SCAR:** Isolated non-conformance; corrective action straightforward; closed within 30 days → No impact to supplier scorecard if rare occurrence
- **Major SCAR:** Repeat issue, systemic quality system failure, safety impact, or delivery impact → Supplier scorecard downgraded to Yellow or Red; may trigger for-cause audit
- **Chronic Issues:** Supplier with ≥3 major SCARs in 12 months → Executive escalation; supplier placed on probation (Section 15.5)

**Preventive Action (PA):**
- Eurus encourages suppliers to identify and address potential issues before they result in non-conformances (proactive mindset)
- Suppliers may initiate Preventive Action Requests (PARs) for process improvements, risk mitigations, or lessons learned from other programs
- Eurus recognizes suppliers with active PA programs in annual Supplier Excellence Awards

---

### 15.5 Supplier Probation & Disqualification

**Probation Criteria:**
- Supplier scorecard Red status for ≥3 consecutive months
- ≥3 major SCARs in 12 months
- Safety-related defect that reaches end customer or causes aircraft grounding
- Failure to meet delivery commitments resulting in program schedule impact (aircraft delivery delay)
- Financial distress (e.g., bankruptcy filing, payment default to sub-tier suppliers)

**Probation Actions:**
- **No New Orders:** Supplier receives no new purchase orders until probation lifted; existing POs continue (unless material risk warrants cancellation)
- **Enhanced Oversight:** Resident

## 1. INTRODUCTION AND SCOPE

### 1.1 Purpose

This Test & Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP) establishes the comprehensive test strategy, objectives, phases, resources, and success criteria for the MAAP-1 program. It serves as the authoritative planning document governing all test activities from component qualification through operational deployment and supports:

- **Verification:** Confirmation that the system is built correctly per specifications
- **Validation:** Confirmation that the correct system was built for the intended missions
- **Certification:** Demonstration of airworthiness and operational safety
- **Risk Reduction:** Early identification and mitigation of technical, safety, and performance risks

### 1.2 Scope and Applicability

This TEMP applies to all test activities conducted on:

- **MAAP-1 Common "Green Aircraft"** (baseline tandem-rotor airframe and dynamic systems)
- **MAAP-1F "Wildfire" Variant** (autonomous aerial firefighting configuration)
- **MAAP-1I "Sentinel" Variant** (ISR mission configuration)
- **MAAP-1C "Atlas" Variant** (cargo/logistics configuration)

The TEMP encompasses:
- Component and subsystem qualification testing
- Ground integration testing (static, dynamic, environmental)
- Developmental flight testing (envelope expansion, performance validation)
- Autonomous system testing (GNC, DAA, swarm operations)
- Payload integration testing (variant-specific mission equipment)
- Operational testing (mission scenarios, user evaluation)
- Certification testing (FAA Part 29, military airworthiness)

### 1.3 Document Relationship and Traceability

This TEMP is derived from and subordinate to:

- **Program Requirements Baseline (PRB):** AF-MAAP1-PRB-0001, Rev A
- **Key Specifications Document (KSD):** AF-MAAP1-KSD-0002, Rev A
- **Integrated Master Schedule (IMS):** MAAP1-SCH-001, Rev A
- **System Safety Program Plan (SSPP):** AF-MAAP1-SSPP-0003, Rev A

All test objectives trace directly to specific PRB requirements via the Requirements Verification Traceability Matrix (RVTM-0001).

## 2. TEST PHILOSOPHY AND STRATEGY

### 2.1 Overall Test Philosophy

The MAAP-1 T&E program is structured around the following core principles:

#### 2.1.1 **Build-Up Approach: Test What You Fly, Fly What You Test**

Progressive complexity from components → subsystems → integrated systems → flight vehicle ensures thorough understanding at each level before advancing to the next. No flight article will proceed to flight test without comprehensive ground validation.

#### 2.1.2 **Early Risk Retirement**

Critical technologies, novel design features, and high-risk subsystems (autonomy, tandem-rotor interference, swarm coordination) undergo accelerated testing early in the program to identify and mitigate issues before they impact critical path milestones.

TEST PHILOSOPHY AND STRATEGY

### 2.1 Overall Test Philosophy

The MAAP-1 T&E program is structured around the following core principles:

#### 2.1.1 **Build-Up Approach: Test What You Fly, Fly What You Test**

Progressive complexity from components → subsystems → integrated systems → flight vehicle ensures thorough understanding at each level before advancing to the next. No flight article will proceed to flight test without comprehensive ground validation.

#### 2.1.2 **Early Risk Retirement**

Critical technologies, novel design features, and high-risk subsystems (autonomy, tandem-rotor interference, swarm coordination) undergo accelerated testing early in the program to identify and mitigate issues before they impact critical path milestones.

#### 2.1.3 **Test-Analyze-Fix-Test (TAFT) Cycle**

Disciplined closed-loop process ensuring anomalies are thoroughly analyzed, root causes identified, corrective actions implemented, and effectiveness verified before proceeding.

#### 2.1.4 **Multi-Variant Efficiency**

Commonality strategy (>80% part count across variants) allows Green Aircraft qualification to satisfy majority of test objectives for all three variants, with focused delta testing for variant-unique systems.

#### 2.1.5 **Integrated DT/OT Approach**

Where feasible, developmental test (DT) and operational test (OT) are conducted concurrently to maximize efficiency while maintaining independence of operational assessment.

#### 2.1.6 **Safety-First Culture**

All test activities prioritize safety of test personnel, flight crew, ground personnel, and the public. Flight test envelope expansion follows conservative incremental approach with well-defined abort criteria and contingency procedures.

### 2.4 Dual-Use Certification Strategy

The MAAP-1 program pursues parallel certification paths:

#### **Civilian Certification Path (MAAP-1F, MAAP-1C)**
- **Authority:** FAA
- **Regulation:** 14 CFR Part 29 (Transport Category Rotorcraft)
- **Special Conditions:** Autonomous operations, Beyond Visual Line of Sight (BVLOS)
- **Certification Basis Agreement:** PM 18 (targeting Q2 2027)
- **Type Certificate Target:** PM 84 (MAAP-1C baseline, Q4 2031)

#### **Military Certification Path (MAAP-1I, MAAP-1F optional)**
- **Authority:** DoD Airworthiness Certification Process
- **Standard:** MIL-HDBK-516C
- **Military Flight Release:** Service-specific requirements
- **Target:** PM 84 (concurrent with FAA TC)

**Commonality Strategy:**
- Green Aircraft certified to most stringent requirements (FAA + MIL)
- Variant-specific systems certified via Type Certificate Amendment (FAA) or supplemental military airworthiness assessment
- Test data shared across certification authorities to minimize duplication
- Joint DT/OT test events where regulatory authorities agree to observe same test

### 2.5 Requirements Verification Strategy

All 487 PRB-derived requirements are assigned verification methods per RVTM-0001:

| Verification Method | % of Requirements | Test Phase(s) |
|---------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Test (T)** | 62% | Phases 1-10 |
| **Analysis (A)** | 23% | Continuous (PDR-CDR-TRR) |
| **Demonstration (D)** | 11% | Phases 6, 9 |
| **Inspection (I)** | 4% | Continuous (design reviews, production) |

**Verification Closure Gates:**
- **PDR Exit:** 100% of requirements allocated to verification method
- **CDR Exit:** 80% of analysis-based requirements closed; test procedures approved
- **First Flight:** 100% of ground-testable requirements verified
- **DT Complete (PM 72):** 95% of KPPs verified to Threshold
- **OT&E Complete (PM 84):** 100% of KPPs verified; 85% of KPPs achieved Objective

### 3.2 Test Article Strategy

**Development Test Articles (DTAs):** Non-flying ground test articles
- **DTA-1:** Static structural test article (full airframe, ultimate load test to failure)
- **DTA-2:** Propulsion integration test article (engine/rotor/transmission ground runs)
- **DTA-3:** Avionics integration test article (Iron Bird, full mission systems)

**Key Roles and Responsibilities:**

### 3.4 Test Governance and Decision Authority

**Test Readiness Reviews (TRRs):** Required before major test phase initiation
- **Attendees:** Program Manager, Chief Engineer, T&E Director, Safety Officer, Customer Representative
- **Exit Criteria:** Test procedures approved, test article configuration controlled, safety case accepted, resources committed

**Test Review Boards (TRBs):** Weekly during active testing
- **Purpose:** Review previous week's results, approve next week's test plan, address anomalies
- **Decision Authority:** T&E Director (routine), Program Manager (significant deviations)

**Anomaly Review Board (ARB):** Convened for any test anomaly
- **Classification:** Category 1 (safety-critical), Category 2 (performance-impacting),

crew training, flight safety authority

- **Classification:** Category 1 (safety-critical), Category 2 (performance-impacting), Category 3 (informational)

- **Authority:** Chief Test Pilot has absolute authority to delay/abort testing for safety concerns

## 4. PHASE 1: COMPONENT & SUBSYSTEM TESTING

**Duration:** 12 months (PM 36-48)  
**Objective:** Qualify critical components and subsystems to design specifications before integration  
**Test Location:** Eurus Systems Component Test Lab; Supplier facilities (witnessed)

### 4.1 Phase Overview and Objectives

Phase 1 testing establishes confidence in individual components and subsystems through qualification testing to design limits and beyond. This phase directly supports:
- **Risk Reduction:** Early identification of component design flaws
- **Design Maturation:** Data-driven refinement before CDR
- **Supplier Validation:** Verification of supplier-provided items
- **Safety Case Development:** Component-level FMEA validation

### 4.2 Test Campaigns

#### 4.2.1 Rotor Blade Qualification

**Test Article:** 4× production-representative composite rotor blades

| Test | Objective | Success Criteria | Duration |
|------|-----------|------------------|----------|
| **Static Load Test** | Verify blade strength to ultimate load | Withstand 150% design limit load without failure | 4 weeks |
| **Fatigue Test** | Demonstrate blade life >5,000 hours | Complete 2× design life cycles without crack initiation | 16 weeks |
| **Whirl Test** | Validate blade balance and track | ≤0.5 in tip tracking error at 268 RPM | 2 weeks |
| **Lightning Strike Test** | Verify lightning protection system | No critical damage after Zone 1A direct strike | 1 week |
| **Bird Strike Test** | Validate erosion protection | No penetration from 4 lb bird @ 150 KTAS | 1 week |
| **Environmental Exposure** | Assess composite durability | <5% strength degradation after 1000 hr salt fog + UV | 8 weeks |

**Total Test Hours:** 1,200 hours  
**Test Completion:** PM 44

#### 4.2.2 Transmission System Qualification

**Test Article:** Full-scale forward transmission, aft transmission, interconnect driveshaft

| Test | Objective | Success Criteria | Duration |
|------|-----------|------------------|----------|
| **Back-to-Back Load Test** | Verify torque capacity | Sustain 150% max continuous torque for 30 min | 3 weeks |
| **Endurance Test** | Demonstrate 5,000 hr TBO | 250 hr accelerated mission profile | 6 weeks |
| **OEI Transient Test** | Validate single-engine operation | 50 cycles of 2.5 min OEI power | 2 weeks |
| **Run-Dry Test** | Verify 30 min oil-out capability | Operate 30 min with total oil loss | 1 week |
| **Vibration Survey** | Characterize vibration signature | Meet MIL-STD-810H vibration limits | 2 weeks |

**Total Test Hours:** 300 hours (powered operation)  
**Test Completion:** PM 46

**Test Article:** 12× primary flight control actuators (triplex redundant set)

| Test | Objective | Success Criteria | Duration |
|------|-----------|------------------|----------|
| **Load Cycle Test** | Verify actuator life | 500,

000 hr TBO | 250 hr accelerated mission profile | 6 weeks |
| **OEI Transient Test** | Validate single-engine operation | 50 cycles of 2.5 min OEI power | 2 weeks |
| **Run-Dry Test** | Verify 30 min oil-out capability | Operate 30 min with total oil loss | 1 week |
| **Vibration Survey** | Characterize vibration signature | Meet MIL-STD-810H vibration limits | 2 weeks |

**Total Test Hours:** 300 hours (powered operation)  
**Test Completion:** PM 46

#### 4.2.3 Flight Control Actuator Qualification

**Test Article:** 12× primary flight control actuators (triplex redundant set)

| Test | Objective | Success Criteria | Duration |

## 5. PHASE 2: GROUND INTEGRATION TESTING

**Duration:** 12 months (PM 42-54, overlaps with Phase 1)  
**Objective:** Integrate and verify subsystems in representative ground test environments  
**Test Location:** Eurus Systems Integration Lab; Iron Bird facility

### 5.1 Phase Overview

Phase 2 brings qualified components together into integrated subsystems and ultimately a complete "Iron Bird" ground test article representing the full aircraft systems architecture without flight structure. Key objectives:

- **Interface Verification:** Validate all mechanical, electrical, and data interfaces per ICDs
- **Functional Integration:** Demonstrate integrated system performance
- **Software Integration:** Flight control laws, autonomy algorithms, mission software
- **Fault Propagation:** Understand system-level effects of component failures
- **Human-Machine Interface:** Validate GCS displays and controls

### 5.2 Iron Bird Configuration

**Iron Bird Article:** DTA-3 (Avionics Integration Test Article)

**Scope:**
- Complete flight control system (actuators, sensors, computers)
- Full avionics suite (navigation, communication, mission systems)
- Propulsion system simulator (engine FADEC, fuel system functional model)
- Rotor system simulator (mechanical load representation)
- Electrical power generation and distribution
- Full Ground Control Station (GCS)

**Instrumentation:**
- 2,500+ data channels
- Real-time data acquisition @ 1000 Hz
- Fault injection capability (software, hardware, data bus)
- 6-DOF motion platform (optional, for advanced GNC testing)

### 5.3 Test Campaigns

#### 5.3.1 Systems Integration & Checkout

**Duration:** 8 weeks (PM 42-44)

| Test | Objective | Success Criteria |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| **Power-On Test** | Verify electrical system | All LRUs power up, no smoke test pass |
| **Data Bus Verification** | Validate network architecture | 100% message throughput, <10 ms latency |
| **Sensor Calibration** | Align all sensors | INS alignment <0.1° attitude error |
| **Control Loop Closure** | Verify FCS architecture | Closed-loop response matches model |

#### 5.3.2 Flight Control System Testing

**Duration:** 12 weeks (PM 44-47)

| Test Campaign | Test Points | Objectives |
|---------------|-------------|------------|
| **Control Law Validation** | 150 test cases | Hover, forward flight, transition modes |
| **Redundancy Management** | 75 failure scenarios | Verify graceful degradation, no single-point failures |
| **Autopilot Modes** | 50 automated sequences | Altitude hold, heading hold, waypoint navigation |
| **Autonomous Takeoff/Landing** | 100 simulated scenarios | Precision landing ±1 m CEP |
| **External Load Compensation** | 25 load configurations | Stability augmentation for 12,000 lb external load |

**Success Criteria:**
- 100% of control modes functional
- <1×10⁻⁶ probability of FCS-induced loss of control (analysis + test)
- Autonomous landing success rate >99% in simulation

000 lb external load |

**Success Criteria:**
- 100% of control modes functional
- <1×10⁻⁶ probability of FCS-induced loss of control (analysis + test)
- Autonomous landing success rate >99% in simulation

#### 5.3.3 Autonomous GNC System Testing

| Test Focus | Test Hours | Key Validation |
|------------|------------|----------------|
| **Waypoint Navigation** | 500 hours | Accuracy <10 m CEP |

## 6. PHASE 3: STATIC STRUCTURAL TESTING

**Duration:** 12 months (PM 42-54, parallel with Phases 1-2)  
**Objective:** Verify airframe structural integrity to ultimate load and fatigue life  
**Test Location:** Eurus Systems Structural Test Facility, Building 450  
**Test Article:** DTA-1 (full-scale static test airframe)

### 6.1 Phase Overview

Static structural testing validates the primary structure design through progressive loading to limit load, ultimate load, and cyclic fatigue. This testing directly supports airworthiness certification and provides critical data for:
- Structural certification per 14 CFR Part 29
- Finite Element Model (FEM) validation
- Fatigue life substantiation
- Fail-safe and damage tolerance analysis

### 6.

PHASE 3: STATIC STRUCTURAL TESTING

**Duration:** 12 months (PM 42-54, parallel with Phases 1-2)  
**Objective:** Verify airframe structural integrity to ultimate load and fatigue life  
**Test Location:** Eurus Systems Structural Test Facility, Building 450  
**Test Article:** DTA-1 (full-scale static test airframe)

### 6.1 Phase Overview

Static structural testing validates the primary structure design through progressive loading to limit load, ultimate load, and cyclic fatigue. This testing directly supports airworthiness certification and provides critical data for:
- Structural certification per 14 CFR Part 29
- Finite Element Model (FEM) validation
- Fatigue life substantiation
- Fail-safe and damage tolerance analysis

### 6.2 DTA-1 Configuration

**Test Article Description:**
- Complete airframe: fuselage, rotor pylons, sponsons, tailcone
- Production-representative composite primary structure
- Instrumented with 3,500+ strain gauges, 250 displacement transducers
- Mounted in custom test fixture with 42-point load introduction system
- High-speed video (10,000 fps) for failure mode capture

**Loads Application System:**
- Hydraulic actuators: 42× servo-controlled, 50 kip capacity each
- Computer-controlled load sequencing
- Real-time structural response monitoring
- Automatic test abort on approaching failure

### 6.3 Test Matrix

#### 6.3.1 Limit Load Test Campaign

**Duration:** 12 weeks (PM 42-45)  
**Objective:** Verify structure withstands 100% design limit load with ≤5% permanent deformation

| Load Case | Description | Load Factor | Test Points |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Symmetric Pull-Up** | +3.5 g maneuver | +3.5 g | 15 increments |
| **Symmetric Push-Over** | -1.0 g maneuver | -1.0 g | 10 increments |
| **Rolling Maneuver** | ±60° bank | ±2.0 g lateral | 12 increments |
| **Asymmetric Flight** | Single rotor failure | Combined loading | 10 cases |
| **External Load Operations** | 16,000 lb sling load | +2.5 g vertical | 15 increments |
| **Landing Impact** | Hard landing, max GW | +2.0 g vertical | 8 cases |
| **Rotor Thrust Limit** | Max rotor thrust | Forward/aft pylons | 12 cases |
| **Ground Handling** | Towing, jacking | Point loads | 8 cases |

**Total Limit Load Test Points:** 90  
**Success Criteria:**
- Zero structural failures
- Permanent deformation <5% at any location
- Strain measurements within 10% of FEM predictions

#### 6.3.2 Ultimate Load Test Campaign

**Duration:** 8 weeks (PM 45-47)  
**Objective:** Demonstrate structure withstands 150% design limit load (ultimate load factor = 1.5)

| Load Case | Ultimate Load Factor | Hold Time | Expected Outcome |
|-----------|----------------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Symmetric Pull-Up** | +5.25 g | 3 seconds | No failure |
| **Symmetric Push-Over** | -1.5 g | 3 seconds | No failure |
| **Rolling Maneuver** | ±3.0 g lateral | 3 seconds | No failure |
| **External Load (Objective)** | +3.75 g w/ 16,000 lb | 3 seconds | No failure |
| **Combined Loading** | Critical combined case | 3 seconds | No failure |
| **Test to Failure** | Progressive loading beyond ultimate | Until failure | Document failure mode |

**Total Ultimate Load Test Points:** 45  
**Success Criteria:**
- Sustain ultimate load (150% limit) for ≥3 seconds without catastrophic failure
- Failure mode (if occurs) is gradual and predictable, not sudden brittle fracture
- Primary load paths remain intact; secondary structure may yield

#### 6.3.3 Fatigue Test Campaign

**Duration:** 24 weeks (PM 47-53, critical path item)  
**Objective:** Demonstrate 2× design life (40,000 flight hours equivalent) without crack initiation

**Fatigue Spectrum:**
- Based on mission profile analysis (60% hover, 30% cruise,

75 g w/ 16,000 lb | 3 seconds | No failure |
| **Combined Loading** | Critical combined case | 3 seconds | No failure |
| **Test to Failure** | Progressive loading beyond ultimate | Until failure | Document failure mode |

**Total Ultimate Load Test Points:** 45  
**Success Criteria:**
- Sustain ultimate load (150% limit) for ≥3 seconds without catastrophic failure
- Failure mode (if occurs) is gradual and predictable, not sudden brittle fracture

#### 6.3.3 Fatigue Test Campaign

**Duration:** 24 weeks (PM 47-53, critical path item)  
**Objective:** Demonstrate 2× design life (40,000 flight hours equivalent) without crack initiation

**Fatigue Spectrum:**
- Based on mission profile analysis (60% hover, 30% cruise, 10% maneuvering)
- Load sequences represent 100-hour mission block
- Accelerated testing: 1 calendar week = 200 flight hour equivalent

| Test Block | Flight Hours Represented | Load Cycles | Inspection Interval |
|------------|--------------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Block 1-40** | 0-8,000 hours | 1.2M cycles | Every 2,000 hr equivalent |
| **Block 41-100** | 8,000-20,000 hours | 1.8M cycles | Every 1,000 hr equivalent |
| **Block 101-200** | 20,000-40,000 hours | 3.0M cycles | Every 500 hr equivalent |

**Total Fatigue Cycles:** 6.0 million  
**Test Duration:** 24 weeks continuous operation

**Inspection Methods:**
- Visual inspection (every block)
- Ultrasonic NDI (every 5,000 hr equivalent)
- Thermography (continuous monitoring during testing)

**Success Criteria:**
- Zero crack initiation before 20,000 flight hour equivalent (1× design life)
- Crack growth analysis if cracks detected between 20,000-40,000 hours
- Meet MIL-STD-1530D damage tolerance requirements

#### 6.3.4 Fail-Safe and Damage Tolerance Testing

**Duration:** 6 weeks (PM 53-54)  
**Objective:** Validate fail-safe design and residual strength with damage

| Test | Damage Introduced | Success Criteria |
|------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Notched Element Test** | 2-inch crack in primary structure | Sustain limit load |
| **Severed Stiffener Test** | Single stiffener cut | Sustain limit load via load redistribution |
| **Corrosion Simulation** | 10% thickness loss (simulated) | Sustain limit load |
| **Battle Damage** | 0.50 cal penetration (simulated) | Sustain limit load |

**Success Criteria:**
- Structure with prescribed damage sustains limit load
- Load redistribution paths confirmed
- Inspection intervals established for damage detection

### 6.4 Phase 3 Deliverables and Success Criteria

| Deliverable | Completion Date | Content |
|-------------|-----------------|---------|
| **Limit Load Test Report** | PM 46 | 90 load cases, strain survey, FEM correlation |
| **Ultimate Load Test Report** | PM 48 | Ultimate load demonstration, margin verification |
| **Fatigue Test Report** | PM 54 | 40,000 hr life demonstration, inspection program |
| **Structural Substantiation Report** | PM 54 | Certification basis compliance (FAA Part 29.305-309) |

**Phase Exit Criteria:**
- 100% of certification load cases tested
- Ultimate load capability demonstrated (150% limit load)
- Fatigue life ≥2× design life (40,000 hours) demonstrated
- FEM correlation within ±10% for 95% of measurement locations
- Damage tolerance and fail-safe characteristics validated
- Zero open Category 1 structural anomalies

**Phase 3 Exit Gate:** Structural Qualification Review (PM 54)

## 7. PHASE 4: PROPULSION GROUND TESTING

**Duration:** 12 months (PM 45-57)

**Test Location:** Eurus Systems Propulsion Test Facility; Engine Manufacturer (selected tests)  
**Test Article:** DTA-2 (propulsion integration test stand)

### 7.1 Phase Overview

Propulsion ground testing validates the complete engine-transmission-rotor system in a ground-based environment before first flight. This phase addresses critical program risks:
- Engine/airframe integration issues
- Rotor system performance and vibration
- Fuel system functionality
- Thermal management effectiveness
- OEI capability verification

### 7.2 DTA-2 Configuration

**Test Stand Description:**
- Forward and aft rotor systems (production-representative)
- Twin turboshaft engines (production engines)
- Full transmission system (forward/aft/interconnect)
- Complete fuel system
- Engine control system (FADEC)

**Thrust Measurement:**

**Safety Systems:**

- Emergency rotor brake

### 7.3 Test Campaigns

#### 7.3.1 Initial System Checkout

**Duration:** 4 weeks (PM 45-46)

| Test | Objective | Success Criteria |

## 8. PHASE 5: ENVIRONMENTAL QUALIFICATION

**Duration:** 12 months (PM 48-60)  
**Objective:** Qualify aircraft systems to full environmental operating envelope  
**Test Location:** Multiple environmental test facilities (Eurus, suppliers, independent labs)  
**Test Articles:** DTA-3 (avionics), environmental test specimens, production LRUs

### 8.1 Phase Overview

Environmental qualification validates system functionality and structural integrity across the full range of environmental conditions specified in PRB Section 3.3 and KSD Section 10:

- Temperature: -40°C to +52°C (objective)
- Altitude: -1,000 ft to +14,000 ft PA
- Humidity: 0% to 95% RH
- Sand, dust, salt fog, rain, icing
- Solar radiation, fungus resistance
- Vibration, shock, acoustic
- Electromagnetic environment (EMI/EMC, lightning, HIRF)

### 8.1 Phase Overview

Environmental qualification validates system functionality and structural integrity across the full range of environmental conditions specified in PRB Section 3.3 and KSD Section 10:

### 8.2 Test Standards and Specifications

**Primary Test Standards:**
- **MIL-STD-810H:** Environmental Engineering Considerations and Laboratory Tests
- **DO-160G:** Environmental Conditions and Test Procedures for Airborne Equipment
- **MIL-STD-464D:** Electromagnetic Environmental Effects (E3) Requirements
- **MIL-STD-461G:** Requirements for the Control of Electromagnetic Interference

### 8.3 Test Campaigns

#### 8.3.1 Temperature Testing

**Test Facility:** Environmental chamber (temp range -54°C to +85°C)

| System Under Test | Low Temp Test | High Temp Test | Operating Test | Success Criteria |
|-------------------|---------------|----------------|----------------|------------------|
| **Avionics LRUs** | -54°C, 4 hr soak | +71°C, 4 hr soak | Functional @ extremes | 100% functionality |
| **Flight Control Actuators** | -40°C | +71°C | Full rate, load capacity | No performance loss |
| **Hydraulic System** | -40°C | +71°C | Pressure, flow per spec | ≤10% performance change |
| **Composite Structure** | -54°C | +85°C | Static load test @ temp | No strength degradation |
| **Battery System** | -20°C | +60°C | Charge/discharge cycles | ≥80% capacity retained |
| **Engine Systems** | -40°C cold start | +52°C hot start | Start within 90 sec | Meet start criteria |

**Test Duration:** 8 weeks (PM 48-50)

**Success Criteria:**
- All systems functional across operating temperature range (-40°C to +52°C objective)
- Non-operating storage: -54°C to +71°C (4-hour soak, no damage)
- Composite structure: No strength loss >5% at temperature extremes

**Lightning Protection Design Features:**
- Composite structure with embedded aluminum mesh (0.125 in spacing)
- Lightning diverter strips on rotor blades (stainless steel)
- Bonding resistance <2.5 mΩ between major assemblies
- Shielded wiring harnesses with 360° terminations
- Transient suppression on all avionics interfaces

**Test Duration:** 8 weeks (PM 58-60)

**Success Criteria:**
- Zero critical system failures during lightning/HIRF exposure
- All flight-critical systems maintain operation or recover within 5 seconds
- No permanent damage requiring more than inspection/minor repair

## 9. PHASE 6: DEVELOPMENTAL FLIGHT TEST

**Duration:** 18 months (PM 54-72)  
**Objective:** Expand flight envelope, verify performance, validate autonomous systems in flight  
**Test Location:** Edwards AFB, CA (primary); China Lake NAWS, CA; Yuma Proving Ground, AZ  
**Test Articles:** FTA-1 (first flight), FTA-2 (envelope expansion), FTA-3 (performance validation)

### 9.1 Phase Overview

Developmental Flight Test (DFT) is the critical validation phase where the MAAP-1 transitions from ground-tested hardware to a flying aircraft demonstrating full operational capability. This phase directly addresses:

- **Airworthiness Certification:** FAA Part 29 and military airworthiness compliance demonstration
- **Flight Envelope Expansion:** Systematic exploration of speed, altitude, and maneuvering limits
- **Performance Validation:** Verification of all KPPs requiring flight test (hover, speed, range, endurance)
- **Autonomous GNC Validation:** Real-world validation of autonomy, DAA, and lost-link procedures
- **Handling Qualities:** Pilot (optionally-piloted variant) and autonomous control law refinement

**Flight Test Philosophy:**
- **Incremental Envelope Expansion:** Small, safe steps from known conditions to envelope limits
- **Build-Up Approach:** Ground test → taxi → hover → low-speed flight → cruise → high-speed → full envelope
- **Redundant Safety:** Chase aircraft, real-time telemetry monitoring, abort procedures for all test points
- **Data-Driven Decisions:** No envelope expansion without thorough analysis of previous test data

### 9.2 Flight Test Organization

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          FLIGHT TEST ORGANIZATION                      │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

                    ┌──────────────────┐
                    │  Flight Test     │

                    └────────┬─────────┘
                             │
        ┌────────────────────┴────────────────────┐
        │                                         │

│ Chief Test Pilot│                      │ Flight Test    │
│                 │                      │ Engineers      │
└───────┬────────┘                       └───────┬────────┘
        │                                         │
    ┌───┴────┬──────┐                    ┌───────┴────┬───────┐
┌───▼───┐ ┌──▼───┐ ┌▼────┐          ┌───▼────┐ ┌─────▼────┐ ┌▼──────┐
│Test   │ │Safety│ │Chase│          │Inst.   │ │Data      │ │Flight │
│Pilots │ │Pilot │ │Pilot│          │Engineer│ │Analysis  │ │Ops    │
└───────┘ └──────┘ └─────┘          └────────┘ └──────────┘ └───────┘
```

**Key Roles:**
- **Flight Test Director:** Overall test program authority, risk acceptance
- **Chief Test Pilot:** Flight safety authority, test execution oversight
- **Test Pilots (3×):** Execute flight test cards, provide qualitative assessment
- **Safety Pilot:** Independent safety observer, emergency authority
- **Flight Test Engineers (12×):** Test planning, data analysis, requirements verification
- **Instrumentation Engineer:** Test instrumentation, data acquisition systems
- **Data Analysts (4×):** Real-time and post-flight data processing
- **Flight Operations (8×):** Aircraft preparation, maintenance, mission planning

### 9.4 Test Campaigns

#### 9.4.1 Phase 6A: First Flight and Initial Envelope (PM 54-60, FTA-1)

**Duration:** 6 months  
**Flight Hours:** 60 hours (45 flights)  
**Test Location:** Edwards AFB

| Test Campaign | Flights | Objectives | Success Criteria |
|---------------|---------|-----------|------------------|
| **Ground Operations** | 0 | Pre-flight validation, taxi tests | Ground handling characteristics acceptable |
